parts.content: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the electrons. (Use $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$, $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$, $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$.)
parts.markscheme:
$E_k = eV = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 400 = 6.4 \times 10^{-17}\ \text{J}$ <marks>1</marks>
$p = \sqrt{2 m_e E_k} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-17}} = 1.08 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{kg m s}^{-1}$ <marks>1</marks>
$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{1.08 \times 10^{-23}} = 6.14 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define the solution path
steps.content:
To find the de Broglie wavelength of an electron, we follow a three-step path:
1. Find the **kinetic energy** gained from the accelerating voltage.
2. Convert that energy into **momentum**.
3. Use the **de Broglie relation** to find the wavelength.

This is a classic problem in **Topic E.2: Quantum physics**.

steps.title: Calculate kinetic energy
steps.content:
When a charge $q$ is accelerated through a potential difference $V$, the work done (and thus the kinetic energy $E_k$ gained) is given by:

$$E_k = qV$$

Substituting the values for an electron:
$$\begin{aligned} E_k &= (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) \times (400 \text{ V}) \\ E_k &= 6.40 \times 10^{-17} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the momentum
steps.content:
Next, we relate kinetic energy to momentum $p$. From **Topic A.3 (Work, energy and power)** and **Topic A.2 (Forces and momentum)**, we know:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \quad \text{and} \quad p = mv$$

By combining these, we get the relationship:
$$p = \sqrt{2mE_k}$$

Plugging in the mass of the electron and our calculated energy:
$$\begin{aligned} p &= \sqrt{2 \times (9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}) \times (6.40 \times 10^{-17} \text{ J})} \\ p &= \sqrt{1.166 \times 10^{-46}} \\ p &\approx 1.08 \times 10^{-23} \text{ kg m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply de Broglie formula
steps.content:
Finally, we use the de Broglie wavelength formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

Using Planck's constant $h$ from the data booklet:
$$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{1.08 \times 10^{-23} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}} \\ \lambda &\approx 6.14 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This wavelength is in the range of X-rays, which explains why electrons can produce diffraction patterns when hitting crystals!

gdc.instructions: Perform the division of Planck's constant by the momentum.

desmos.instructions: Divide the value of h by the calculated p.

calculator.description: Calculate the final de Broglie wavelength using the momentum value.

steps.title: Identify the approach
steps.content:
To find the de Broglie wavelength of the electrons, we will use the **Intermediate Velocity Approach**. This involves three main stages:
1. Find the kinetic energy ($E_k$) gained by the electrons from the accelerating potential.
2. Calculate the velocity ($v$) of the electrons from that kinetic energy.
3. Substitute the velocity into the de Broglie equation to find the wavelength ($\lambda$).

This topic is covered in **Topic E.2: Quantum physics**, which explores the wave-particle duality of matter.

highlights.text: accelerated through a potential difference of $400\ \text{V}$

steps.title: Calculate kinetic energy
steps.content:
When a charge $q$ is accelerated through a potential difference $V$, the work done (which becomes its kinetic energy) is given by $W = qV$. For an electron, the charge is $e$.

$$E_k = eV$$

Substituting the values:
$$E_k = (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) \times (400 \text{ V})$$
$$E_k = 6.40 \times 10^{-17} \text{ J}$$

This calculation earns the first mark on the markscheme.

calculator.description: Calculate the kinetic energy in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate electron velocity
steps.content:
Now, we relate the kinetic energy to the velocity using the formula from the Mechanics section of your data booklet:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Rearranging for velocity ($v$):
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2E_k}{m_e}}$$

Substituting our energy and the mass of the electron:
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.40 \times 10^{-17} \text{ J}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}}}$$
$$v \approx 1.185 \times 10^7 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Solve for velocity using the square root of (2 * energy / mass).

steps.title: Determine de Broglie wavelength
steps.content:
Finally, we use the de Broglie relation $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$, where momentum $p = mv$. Combining these gives:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{m_ev}$$

Using Planck's constant $h$ and our calculated velocity:
$$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}) \times (1.185 \times 10^7 \text{ m s}^{-1})}$$
$$\lambda \approx 6.14 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$$

This matches the final value required in the markscheme for the third mark.

calculator.description: Calculate wavelength using h / (m * v).

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The de Broglie wavelength of the electrons is:

$$\lambda = 6.14 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$$

(Note: This is approximately $0.0614 \text{ nm}$, which is comparable to the spacing of atoms in a crystal, explaining why diffraction occurs.)

steps.title: Identify required physics concepts
steps.content:
To find the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated by a potential difference, we need to link its wave properties to its energy. This problem involves concepts from **Topic E.2: Quantum Physics** (wave-particle duality) and **Topic D.3: Motion in electromagnetic fields**.

We are given:
- Potential difference $V = 400\ \text{V}$
- Electron mass $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$
- Planck's constant $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$
- Elementary charge $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$

steps.title: Combine into one formula
steps.content:
Instead of solving in multiple stages, we can derive a single expression for the wavelength $\lambda$. 

1. The kinetic energy ($E_k$) gained by an electron in an electric field is $E_k = eV$.
2. The relationship between kinetic energy and momentum ($p$) is $E_k = \frac{p^2}{2m}$, which rearranges to $p = \sqrt{2m E_k}$.
3. The de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$.

Substituting the energy into the momentum equation, and then the momentum into the wavelength equation, we get:
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into our combined formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 400}}$$

Using a calculator, we find the denominator first (the momentum):
$$p \approx 1.08 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{kg m s}^{-1}$$

Then divide $h$ by this value to get $\lambda$.

calculator.description: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength using the combined formula.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The result of the calculation is:

$$\lambda = 6.14 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}$$

In an IB exam, marks are awarded for identifying the correct kinetic energy ($1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 400$), calculating the momentum, and providing the final wavelength with correct units and significant figures.

parts.content: Using Bragg’s law $n \lambda = 2 d \sin \theta$ with $n=1$, calculate the lattice spacing $d$ of the crystal.
parts.markscheme:
Substitution: $\frac{6.14 \times 10^{-11}}{2 \sin 15^\circ}$ <marks>1</marks>
$d = 1.19 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate de Broglie Wavelength
steps.content:
To find the lattice spacing, we first need the de Broglie wavelength ($\lambda$) of the electrons. Electrons accelerated through a potential difference $V$ gain kinetic energy $E_k = eV$. This relates to wavelength by:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$$

Using the values from the data booklet:
- $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$
- $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$
- $V = 400 \text{ V}$

$$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 400}} \approx 6.14 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$$

highlights.text: accelerated through a potential difference of 400\ V

steps.title: Rearrange Bragg's Law
steps.content:
We are given Bragg's Law for constructive interference and asked to solve for the lattice spacing $d$. 

Starting with the equation:
$$n \lambda = 2d \sin \theta$$

To isolate $d$, we divide both sides by $2 \sin \theta$:
$$d = \frac{n \lambda}{2 \sin \theta}$$

steps.title: Substitute and Calculate
steps.content:
Now we substitute the known values into the rearranged equation:
- $n = 1$ (first maximum)
- $\lambda = 6.14 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$
- $\theta = 15^\circ$

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \frac{1 \times 6.14 \times 10^{-11}}{2 \sin 15^\circ} \\ d &= 1.186... \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $d = 1.19 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$.

steps.title: Identify required variables
steps.content: To find the lattice spacing $d$, we must link the accelerating potential difference to the diffraction condition. We are given the potential difference $V = 400\text{ V}$ and the diffraction angle $\theta = 15^\circ$ for the first maximum ($n=1$). We will also need the Planck constant $h$, the charge of an electron $e$, and the mass of an electron $m_e$ from the data booklet.

steps.title: Derive the master equation
steps.content:
Using the **Combined Algebraic Approach**, we derive a single formula for $d$. 

1. The kinetic energy of the electron is $E_k = eV$. 
2. Momentum is related to kinetic energy by $p = \sqrt{2m_e E_k} = \sqrt{2m_e eV}$.
3. The de Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e eV}}$.
4. Substitute this into Bragg’s law $n\lambda = 2d \sin \theta$:

$$n \left( \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e eV}} \right) = 2d \sin \theta$$

Solving for $d$ gives the master equation:

$$d = \frac{nh}{2 \sin \theta \sqrt{2m_e eV}}$$

steps.title: Single-step calculation
steps.content:
Now, we substitute all values and constants into our master equation:
- $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J s}$
- $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$
- $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\text{ kg}$
- $n = 1, V = 400, \theta = 15^\circ$

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \frac{1 \cdot 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \cdot \sin(15^\circ) \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 400}} \\ d &\approx 1.19 \times 10^{-10}\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme requirement by correctly substituting the effective wavelength into the spacing formula.

parts.content: Explain how the electron diffraction experiment provides evidence for the wave nature of matter.
parts.markscheme:
Electron diffraction pattern shows interference effects typical of waves <marks>1</marks>
Particles behaving like waves confirms wave–particle duality of matter <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify wave-exclusive behaviors
steps.content:
In physics, certain phenomena such as **diffraction** and **interference** are considered signature behaviors of waves. Diffraction occurs when a wave encounters an obstacle or a gap, causing it to bend and spread out. When these spread-out waves overlap, they interfere with one another to create a pattern of maxima and minima. 

These concepts are explored in detail in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**.

steps.title: Connect patterns to electrons
steps.content: The question states that the electron beam produces a "diffraction pattern". Because electrons (which have mass and charge) are producing a pattern that is characteristic of interference, we must conclude that they are behaving as waves in this context. Particles, in a classical sense, would simply pass through or bounce off the crystal without forming an interference pattern.

steps.title: Conclude wave-particle duality
steps.content:
The observation of these wave-like interference effects from a beam of particles provides direct evidence for **wave–particle duality**. This confirms that matter, such as an electron, possesses a dual nature and can exhibit wave properties under specific conditions, as suggested by the de Broglie hypothesis.

According to the markscheme, you earn marks for:
1. Stating that the diffraction pattern shows interference effects typical of waves.
2. Explaining that particles behaving like waves confirms the wave–particle duality of matter.

steps.title: Recognize diffraction as wave behavior
steps.content:
In physics, diffraction and interference are phenomena exclusive to waves. When a beam of electrons is directed at a crystal and produces a **diffraction pattern** (areas of constructive and destructive interference), it demonstrates that the electrons are not just behaving like tiny 'billiard ball' particles, but are propagating as waves.

This relates back to **Topic C.3 (Wave phenomena)**, where we learn that waves spread out and interfere when they encounter obstacles or openings comparable to their wavelength.

steps.title: Apply the de Broglie hypothesis
steps.content:
The de Broglie hypothesis suggests that every moving particle has an associated wavelength, known as the de Broglie wavelength. This is mathematically expressed as:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

Where $h$ is Planck's constant and $p$ is the momentum of the electron ($p = mv$). 

Because the electrons in this experiment are accelerated to a specific momentum, they acquire a wavelength similar to the spacing between atoms in the crystal. The resulting pattern confirms **wave–particle duality**: the idea that matter, which we usually think of as particles, also possesses wave-like properties. 

You can find more about this in **Topic E.2 (Quantum physics)**.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks for this question, your explanation should clearly state:
1.  The observed **diffraction pattern** is evidence of interference, which is a characteristic wave effect.
2.  The fact that particles (electrons) exhibit this behavior confirms the **wave-particle duality** of matter as proposed by de Broglie.

steps.title: Identify diffraction as wave behavior
steps.content: In physics, diffraction and interference are phenomena that are unique to waves. When a beam—whether it's light or matter—passes through a structure like a crystal lattice and spreads out to form a pattern of intensity maxima and minima, it is demonstrating wave-like properties. This concept is explored in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena** and **Topic E.2: Quantum physics**.

steps.title: Compare with X-ray patterns
steps.content: X-rays are known to be electromagnetic waves. When X-rays are directed at a crystal, they produce a specific diffraction pattern due to their interaction with the regular spacing of atoms. By comparing the results, we find that the electron beam produces a nearly identical interference pattern to that of X-rays. This similarity provides direct evidence that electrons possess a wavelength and behave as waves when interacting with the crystal.

steps.title: Confirm wave-particle duality
steps.content:
Because the electrons exhibit interference effects typical of waves, this experiment confirms the principle of **wave–particle duality**. It proves that particles (matter) can behave like waves, which is a fundamental pillar of quantum mechanics. 

In an IB exam, you earn the marks by:
1. Stating that the diffraction pattern shows interference effects typical of waves.
2. Explaining that particles behaving like waves confirms the wave–particle duality of matter.

questions.specification:
A monochromatic light source of frequency $f$ is incident on a metal surface. The number of photons striking the surface per second is $n$, and this results in the emission of photoelectrons with a maximum kinetic energy $E_{k}$ and a total emission rate $R$.

The frequency of the light source is then increased to $2f$ and the number of photons incident per second is increased to $2n$. Which of the following correctly describes the new maximum kinetic energy and the new rate of photoelectron emission?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic E - Nuclear and quantum physics > E.2 Quantum physics (HL only) > E.2.1 Wave–particle duality (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: E.2.1 Wave–particle duality (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question pertains to the photoelectric effect, which is a fundamental demonstration of the particle nature of light and is categorized under wave-particle duality. It requires application of Einstein's photoelectric equation and the relationship between photon flux and emission rate.

options.content: The maximum kinetic energy is equal to $2E_{k}$ and the emission rate is equal to $2R$.
options.markscheme: This assumes a direct proportionality between frequency and maximum kinetic energy ($E_k \propto f$), which ignores the constant work function subtraction in Einstein's equation ($E_k = hf - \Phi$).

options.content: The maximum kinetic energy is greater than $2E_{k}$ and the emission rate is equal to $2R$.
options.markscheme:
- From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $E_k = hf - \Phi$, where $\Phi$ is the work function.
- When frequency is doubled ($2f$), the new kinetic energy $E'$ is $h(2f) - \Phi = 2hf - \Phi$.
- Rearranging the initial equation gives $hf = E_k + \Phi$, so $E' = 2(E_k + \Phi) - \Phi = 2E_k + \Phi$.
- Since $\Phi > 0$, the new kinetic energy $E'$ is greater than $2E_k$.
- The rate of emission $R$ is directly proportional to the number of incident photons per second $n$ (assuming the frequency is above the threshold). Since $n$ is doubled, $R$ is doubled.

options.content: The maximum kinetic energy is equal to $2E_{k}$ and the emission rate is greater than $2R$.
options.markscheme: This incorrectly identifies the relationship between frequency and kinetic energy as proportional and implies a non-linear relationship between photon rate and emission rate.

options.content: The maximum kinetic energy is greater than $2E_{k}$ and the emission rate is greater than $2R$.

parts.content: Calculate the Compton wavelength shift $\Delta \lambda$ for the scattered photons. (Use $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$, $c = 3.00 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m s}^{-1}$.)
parts.markscheme:
Calculate Compton wavelength: $\frac{h}{m_e c} = 2.43 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Calculate shift: $\Delta \lambda = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} (1 - \cos 60^\circ) = 1.215 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$ (or $0.00122\ \text{nm}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the formula and constants
steps.content:
To find the wavelength shift in Compton scattering, we use the Compton scattering formula:

$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos \theta)$$

where:
- $h$ is the Planck constant
- $m_e$ is the rest mass of an electron
- $c$ is the speed of light
- $\theta$ is the scattering angle

steps.title: Calculate the Compton wavelength
steps.content:
The term $\frac{h}{m_e c}$ is known as the Compton wavelength of the electron. Let's calculate its value first:

$$\frac{h}{m_e c} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}$$

$$\frac{h}{m_e c} \approx 2.43 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$$

This calculation earns you the first mark (M1).

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the Compton wavelength.

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength shift
steps.content:
Now, substitute the value of $\frac{h}{m_e c}$ and the scattering angle $\theta = 60^\circ$ into the formula to find $\Delta \lambda$:

$$\Delta \lambda = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} (1 - \cos 60^\circ)$$

Since $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$:

$$\Delta \lambda = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} (1 - 0.5)$$
$$\Delta \lambda = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.5$$
$$\Delta \lambda = 1.215 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$$

In nanometers ($1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m}$), this is approximately $0.00122 \text{ nm}$.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the calculated Compton wavelength by (1 - cos(60)). Check degree mode.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous result by (1 - cos(60)). Ensure the calculator is in degree mode.

desmos.instructions: Enter (2.426e-12) * (1 - cos(60°)) in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final shift in meters.

parts.content: Determine the wavelength of the scattered photons.
parts.markscheme:
$\lambda_f = \lambda_i + \Delta \lambda = 0.030 + 0.001215 = 0.0312\ \text{nm}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the scattering process
steps.content: In this problem, we are looking at **Compton scattering**, where a photon hits an electron and loses some energy, resulting in a longer wavelength. We are given the incident wavelength and the scattering angle, and we need to find the new wavelength of the photon after the collision.

steps.title: Recall the Compton formula
steps.content:
To find the change in wavelength ($Δ\lambda$), we use the Compton scattering formula found in **Topic E.2: Quantum physics (HL)**:

$$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_f - \lambda_i = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos\theta)$$

Where:
- $h$ is Planck's constant ($6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$)
- $m_e$ is the mass of the electron ($9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$)
- $c$ is the speed of light ($3.00 \times 10^8\ \text{m s}^{-1}$)
- $\theta$ is the scattering angle

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength shift
steps.content:
First, we calculate the wavelength shift $\Delta\lambda$. Substituting the constants and the given angle $\theta = 60^\circ$:

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(3.00 \times 10^8)} (1 - \cos 60^\circ)$$

Since $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$, the term $(1 - \cos 60^\circ)$ becomes $0.5$. Calculating this gives:

$$\Delta\lambda \approx 1.213 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$$

To match the units of the incident wavelength, we convert this to nanometers ($1\ \text{nm} = 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$):

$$\Delta\lambda \approx 0.001213\ \text{nm}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the fraction of constants multiplied by (1 - cos(60)). Ensure degree mode is active.

ti84.instructions: Enter (6.63*10^-34 / (9.11*10^-31 * 3*10^8)) * (1 - cos(60)). Ensure your calculator is in Degree mode.

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula using scientific notation and ensure the angle setting is in degrees.

calculator.description: Calculate the Compton wavelength shift in meters.

steps.title: Determine final wavelength
steps.content:
Now, we add this shift to the initial wavelength $\lambda_i = 0.030\ \text{nm}$ to find the scattered wavelength $\lambda_f$:

$$\lambda_f = \lambda_i + \Delta\lambda$$
$$\lambda_f = 0.030\ \text{nm} + 0.001213\ \text{nm}$$
$$\lambda_f \approx 0.0312\ \text{nm}$$

According to the markscheme, the final answer is $0.0312\ \text{nm}$ (rounded to three decimal places).

gdc.instructions: Add the initial wavelength to the calculated shift.

ti84.instructions: Add 0.030 to the previous result (scaled by 10^9).

desmos.instructions: Add 0.030 to the value of Delta lambda in nm.

calculator.description: Add the shift to the initial wavelength.

parts.content: Calculate the energy of the scattered photons in electronvolts (eV).
parts.markscheme:
Calculate energy in Joules: $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda_f} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}{0.0312 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.37 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{J}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Convert to electronvolts: $E = \frac{6.37 \times 10^{-15}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}} = 39.8\ \text{keV}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Explain the significance of the Compton effect for the development of quantum physics.
parts.markscheme:
The Compton effect shows photons carry momentum / behave as particles <marks>R1</marks>

It provided decisive evidence for the particle nature of light / supports quantum theory over classical wave theory <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Model as a particle collision
steps.content: The Compton effect is modeled as an elastic collision between a photon and a stationary electron. This approach utilizes the principles of conservation of momentum and energy, which are typically applied to discrete particles (like billiard balls) in **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Demonstrate photon momentum
steps.content:
In this collision, the incident photon transfers a portion of its momentum to the electron. Because the photon loses energy and momentum, its wavelength increases. This interaction provides direct evidence that light possesses momentum, defined by the relationship:

$$p = \frac{h}{\lambda}$$

Since momentum is a property classically associated with mass-bearing particles, this confirms that photons behave as discrete particles during interactions with matter.

highlights.text: The Compton effect shows photons carry momentum / behave as particles

steps.title: Challenge classical wave theory
steps.content: The significance lies in the fact that classical wave theory (covered in **Topic C**) could not explain why the wavelength would change; it predicted that the electron should simply oscillate and re-radiate light at the *same* frequency. By proving the particle nature of light, the Compton effect provided the necessary evidence to support quantum theory and the concept of wave-particle duality found in **Topic E.2 Quantum physics**.

highlights.text: It provided decisive evidence for the particle nature of light / supports quantum theory over classical wave theory

steps.title: Classical Wave Theory Prediction
steps.content:
In classical physics, light is treated strictly as an electromagnetic wave. According to classical wave theory, when an EM wave hits an electron, the electron should oscillate at the same frequency as the incoming wave and re-radiate light at that *exact same* frequency. 

Therefore, classical theory predicts that the scattered wavelength should be identical to the incident wavelength ($0.030\text{ nm}$), regardless of the scattering angle.

steps.title: The Observed Contradiction
steps.content: The Compton effect showed that the scattered light actually has a **longer wavelength** than the incident light. In this experiment, the shift in wavelength at $60^\circ$ cannot be explained by waves. This result directly contradicted classical physics, proving that the wave model alone is insufficient to describe the interaction between light and matter.

steps.title: Photons and Momentum
steps.content:
To explain the results, we must treat light as a stream of particles called **photons**. In this model, the interaction is viewed as a collision between a photon and an electron, similar to billiard balls. 

Because the photon has a change in wavelength, it must have transferred some of its energy and momentum to the electron. This demonstrates that **photons carry momentum** and behave like particles.

highlights.text: photons carry momentum / behave as particles

steps.title: Significance for Quantum Physics
steps.content:
The significance of the Compton effect is that it provided **decisive evidence for the particle nature of light**. While the photoelectric effect showed light energy is quantized ($E=hf$), the Compton effect proved light also carries momentum ($p = \frac{h}{\lambda}$).

This supported the development of **quantum theory** and the concept of wave-particle duality, marking a fundamental shift away from classical mechanics.

highlights.text: decisive evidence for the particle nature of light / supports quantum theory over classical wave theory

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This is a photoelectric effect problem: using photon energy (from wavelength) minus metal work function to find maximum electron kinetic energy and then the stopping potential.

parts.content: Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons when illuminated.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the photon energy: $$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}{300 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.63 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Convert energy to eV: $$6.63 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 4.14 \text{ eV}$$

- Calculate maximum kinetic energy: $$E_{k,\text{max}} = 4.14 - 2.5 = 1.64 \text{ eV}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{k,\text{max}}$) of the emitted electrons, we use Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$$E_{k,\text{max}} = hf - \Phi$$

Where:
- $\Phi$ is the work function: $2.5 \text{ eV}$
- $hf$ (or $E_{\text{photon}}$) is the energy of the incoming photon.
- The wavelength of the light $\lambda$ is $300 \text{ nm} = 300 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$.

This concept is a key part of **Topic E: Nuclear and quantum physics**.

steps.title: Calculate photon energy (Joules)
steps.content:
First, we calculate the energy of the incoming photon in Joules. Using the wave equation $$v = f\lambda$$ (where $v = c$) and the energy equation $$E = hf$$, we can combine them to get:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Substituting the constants from the data booklet:
$$E = \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3.00 \times 10^{8})}{300 \times 10^{-9}}$$
$$E = 6.63 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Convert energy to electron-volts
steps.content:
Since the work function is given in electron-volts (eV), it is easier to convert our photon energy from Joules to eV. 

We divide the energy in Joules by the elementary charge $e$:
$$E_{\text{eV}} = \frac{E_{\text{Joules}}}{e}$$

$$E_{\text{eV}} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-19}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 4.14 \text{ eV}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the division operation with scientific notation.

ti84.instructions: Enter the photon energy and divide by the elementary charge constant.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to calculate the energy in eV.

calculator.description: Convert photon energy from Joules to electron-volts.

steps.title: Solve for maximum kinetic energy
steps.content:
Finally, we apply Einstein's photoelectric equation by subtracting the work function from the photon energy:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,\text{max}} &= E_{\text{photon}} - \Phi \\ E_{k,\text{max}} &= 4.14 \text{ eV} - 2.5 \text{ eV} \\ E_{k,\text{max}} &= 1.64 \text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$

This result represents the maximum energy an electron can have when ejected from the metal surface.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the work function from the energy in eV.

ti84.instructions: Subtract 2.5 from the previously calculated value (4.14).

calculator.description: Subtract the work function from the photon energy.

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To find the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons, we first note the values provided in the question:
- Work function ($\Phi$) = $2.5\text{ eV}$
- Wavelength ($\lambda$) = $300\text{ nm} = 300 \times 10^{-9}\text{ m}$

We will use the **Calculation in Joules** approach. This means we'll convert everything to Joules first, solve the photoelectric equation, and then convert the final answer back to electron-volts (eV).

steps.title: Convert work function to Joules
steps.content:
First, we convert the metal's work function from eV to Joules. Using the elementary charge $e$ from the data booklet:

$$1\text{ eV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Phi &= 2.5 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \\ &= 4.0 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate photon energy
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the energy of the incident photons using the formula from the data booklet. Since we are given wavelength, we combine $E = hf$ and $v = f\lambda$ (where $v = c$) to get:

$$\text{Photon Energy } (E) = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Substituting the constants $h$ and $c$:

$$\begin{aligned} E &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}{300 \times 10^{-9}} \\ &= 6.63 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the formula into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the values for h, c, and lambda.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate the energy of the photon in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate kinetic energy (J)
steps.content:
According to the photoelectric effect equation, the maximum kinetic energy is the difference between the photon energy and the work function:

$$E_{k,\text{max}} = E - \Phi$$

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,\text{max}} &= 6.63 \times 10^{-19} - 4.0 \times 10^{-19} \\ &= 2.63 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Convert result to eV
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the kinetic energy back into electron-volts by dividing by the elementary charge $e$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,\text{max}} (\text{in eV}) &= \frac{2.63 \times 10^{-19}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}} \\ &\approx 1.64\text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Divide the energy in Joules by the elementary charge.

calculator.description: Convert the kinetic energy from Joules to eV.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons is **$1.64\text{ eV}$**.

In an IB exam, you would earn one mark for correctly calculating the photon energy ($6.63 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J}$ or $4.14\text{ eV}$) and a second mark for correctly subtracting the work function to find the final kinetic energy.

parts.content: Find the stopping potential needed to stop the emitted electrons.
parts.markscheme:

<note>Accept 1.6 V</note>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
To find the stopping potential, we first need to determine the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons ($E_{k, \text{max}}$). The stopping potential ($V_s$) in Volts is numerically equal to the maximum kinetic energy expressed in electronvolts (eV). 

We will use the photoelectric effect equation:

$$E_{hf} = \Phi + E_{k, \text{max}}$$

where $E_{hf}$ is the energy of the incident photon and $\Phi$ is the work function of the metal.

steps.title: Calculate photon energy (Joules)
steps.content:
First, we calculate the energy of the incident photons using the wavelength $\lambda = 300\text{ nm} = 300 \times 10^{-9}\text{ m}$. From the data booklet, we combine $E = hf$ and $v = f\lambda$ to get:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Substituting the constants:

$$E = \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3.00 \times 10^8)}{300 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E = 6.63 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the energy of the photon in Joules.

steps.title: Convert energy to electronvolts
steps.content:
Since the work function is given in electronvolts (eV), it is easier to convert our photon energy from Joules to eV. We divide by the elementary charge $e$:

$$E_{\text{eV}} = \frac{E_{\text{J}}}{e} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-19}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$E_{\text{eV}} \approx 4.14 \text{ eV}$$

desmos.instructions: Divide the result from step 1 by 1.6*10^-19.

calculator.description: Convert photon energy from Joules to electronvolts.

steps.title: Calculate max kinetic energy
steps.content:
Now we find the maximum kinetic energy by subtracting the work function ($\Phi = 2.5 \text{ eV}$) from the photon energy:

$$E_{k, \text{max}} = E_{\text{eV}} - \Phi$$

$$E_{k, \text{max}} = 4.14 \text{ eV} - 2.5 \text{ eV} = 1.64 \text{ eV}$$

calculator.description: Subtract the work function to find maximum kinetic energy.

steps.title: Determine stopping potential
steps.content:
The stopping potential $V_s$ is the potential difference required to stop the most energetic electrons. It is related to the maximum kinetic energy by the equation $E_{k, \text{max}} = eV_s$.

Because $E_{k, \text{max}}$ is $1.64 \text{ eV}$, the potential required to stop these electrons is simply:

$$V_s = 1.64 \text{ V}$$

steps.title: Convert work function to Joules
steps.content:
To solve this using SI units, we first convert the work function ($\Phi$) from electron-volts (eV) to Joules (J). We use the elementary charge $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ as the conversion factor.

$$\Phi = 2.5 \text{ eV} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV} = 4.0 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Convert the work function to Joules.

steps.title: Calculate photon energy
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the energy of the incident photons ($E_{ph}$) using the wavelength $\lambda = 300 \text{ nm}$. We combine the wave equation $v = f\lambda$ and the energy equation $E = hf$ to get:

$$E_{ph} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Substituting the constants $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ and $c = 3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$:

$$E_{ph} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}{300 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.63 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the energy of the photon in Joules.

steps.title: Find maximum kinetic energy
steps.content:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{k, max}$) of the emitted electrons is the difference between the photon energy and the work function:

$$E_{k, max} = E_{ph} - \Phi$$

$$E_{k, max} = 6.63 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} - 4.00 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 2.63 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Subtract the work function from the photon energy.

steps.title: Calculate the stopping potential
steps.content:
The stopping potential ($V_s$) is the voltage required to stop the most energetic electrons. It is related to the maximum kinetic energy by the equation $E_{k, max} = eV_s$. 

Rearranging for $V_s$:

$$V_s = \frac{E_{k, max}}{e} = \frac{2.63 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}} \approx 1.64 \text{ V}$$

This calculation ensures we account for all energy transfers in standard SI units before finding the final potential difference.

steps.title: Define threshold frequency
steps.content: In the photoelectric effect, electrons are only emitted from a metal surface if the incoming light has enough energy. The **threshold frequency** ($f_0$) is defined as the minimum frequency of incident electromagnetic radiation required to liberate a photoelectron from the surface of a metal.

steps.title: State the formula
steps.content:
The energy of a single photon is given by the equation $E = hf$. For an electron to be emitted, the photon's energy must be at least equal to the metal's **work function** ($\Phi$), which is the minimum energy required to remove an electron.

The relationship between threshold frequency and the work function is:

$$f_0 = \frac{\Phi}{h}$$

Where:
- $f_0$ is the threshold frequency (Hz)
- $\Phi$ is the work function (J)
- $h$ is Planck's constant

steps.title: Final check for marks
steps.content: To earn the mark, you must provide both the qualitative definition (minimum frequency) and the mathematical relationship ($f_0 = \frac{\Phi}{h}$). This question relates to **Topic E.2: Quantum physics (HL only)** or general atomic models, where wave-particle duality and the discrete nature of light are explored.

steps.title: Understanding the Energy Balance
steps.content:
In the photoelectric effect, when a photon hits a metal surface, its energy is transferred to an electron. This process follows the principle of conservation of energy. For an electron to be emitted, the energy of the incident photon ($hf$) must be equal to the energy required to liberate the electron (the work function, $\Phi$) plus any kinetic energy ($E_{k}$) the electron carries away.

The energy balance equation is:
$$hf = \Phi + E_{k,max}$$

steps.title: Define threshold frequency
steps.content:
The **threshold frequency** ($f_0$) is the minimum frequency of light required to emit a photoelectron from a metal surface. 

Using the energy balance approach, this occurs when the photon energy is exactly equal to the work function, leaving the emitted electron with zero kinetic energy ($E_{k,max} = 0$).

steps.title: State the relationship
steps.content:
Based on the energy balance at the threshold point ($hf_0 = \Phi$), we can state the mathematical relationship between the threshold frequency and the work function:

$$f_0 = \frac{\Phi}{h}$$

where:
- $f_0$ is the threshold frequency (Hz)
- $\Phi$ is the work function (J)
- $h$ is Planck's constant

parts.content: Calculate the energy of the incident photons. Use $E = hf$ where $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$.

steps.title: Identify the photon energy formula
steps.content:
To find the energy of a photon, we use the quantum relationship between energy and frequency. This formula is found in the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet.

$$E = hf$$

Where:
- $E$ is the energy in Joules ($J$)
- $h$ is Planck's constant
- $f$ is the frequency in Hertz ($Hz$)

steps.title: Calculate energy in Joules
steps.content:
First, we substitute the given frequency and the value for Planck's constant from the **Physical Constants** section into the formula.

$$\begin{aligned} E &= (6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (8.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}) \\ E &= 5.304 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

In an exam, keeping this to three significant figures ($5.30 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$) would earn you the first mark.

gdc.instructions: Enter the product of Planck's constant and frequency.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation using scientific notation (EE key).

desmos.instructions: Multiply Planck's constant by the given frequency.

calculator.description: Calculate the energy in Joules.

steps.title: Convert Joules to electronvolts
steps.content:
To convert energy from Joules to electronvolts (eV), we divide by the elementary charge constant, $e$. This value is also in the **Physical Constants** section.

$$1 \text{ eV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

Using our previous result:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{eV}} &= \frac{E_{\text{Joules}}}{e} \\ E_{\text{eV}} &= \frac{5.304 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}} \\ E_{\text{eV}} &\approx 3.315 \text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$

Following the markscheme, we round this to 3 significant figures: **3.31 eV**.

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: To find the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons, we use Einstein's photoelectric equation. In this method, we will first calculate the energy of the incident light (photons) in Joules, convert it to electronvolts (eV), and then subtract the work function.

highlights.text: Determine the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons.

steps.title: Calculate photon energy (Joules)
steps.content:
First, we calculate the energy of a single incident photon using the frequency provided. From the **Nuclear** section of the data booklet, we use the formula:

$$E = hf$$

Given:
- $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ (Planck's constant)
- $f = 8.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$

$$E = (6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (8.0 \times 10^{14}) = 5.304 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Convert energy to electronvolts
steps.content:
Since the work function is given in electronvolts (eV), it is easier to convert our photon energy from Joules to eV. We do this by dividing by the elementary charge $e$.

$$E_{\text{eV}} = \frac{E_{\text{Joules}}}{e}$$

Using $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$:

$$E_{\text{eV}} = \frac{5.304 \times 10^{-19}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.315 \text{ eV}$$

Rounding to two decimal places (as seen in the markscheme), we get $3.31 \text{ eV}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the division of the two values in scientific notation.

desmos.instructions: Divide the photon energy by the elementary charge.

calculator.description: Convert photon energy from Joules to eV.

steps.title: Subtract the work function
steps.content:
Finally, we find the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{\max}$) by subtracting the work function ($\Phi$) from the photon energy ($hf$).

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\max} &= hf - \Phi \\ E_{\max} &= 3.31 \text{ eV} - 2.5 \text{ eV} \\ E_{\max} &= 0.81 \text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation shows that the photon has $3.31 \text{ eV}$ of energy; $2.5 \text{ eV}$ is used to liberate the electron, and the remaining $0.81 \text{ eV}$ becomes the electron's kinetic energy.

steps.title: Convert work function to Joules
steps.content:
To solve this problem using Joules, we first need to convert the work function ($\Phi$) from electronvolts (eV) to Joules (J). We use the conversion factor from the data booklet: $1\text{ eV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \Phi &= 2.5 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \\ \Phi &= 4.0 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Next, we calculate the energy of the incident photon ($E$) in Joules using the frequency given in the question and Planck's constant ($h$) from the data booklet.

$$E = hf$$

$$\begin{aligned} E &= (6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (8.0 \times 10^{14}) \\ E &= 5.304 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find maximum kinetic energy
steps.content:
According to the photoelectric effect equation, the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{\max}$) is the difference between the incident photon energy and the work function of the metal.

$$E_{\max} = hf - \Phi$$

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\max} &= 5.304 \times 10^{-19} - 4.0 \times 10^{-19} \\ E_{\max} &= 1.304 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Subtract the work function from the photon energy.

steps.title: Convert energy back to eV
steps.content:
Finally, we convert our answer from Joules back to electronvolts by dividing by $1.60 \times 10^{-19}$.

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\max} (\text{eV}) &= \frac{1.304 \times 10^{-19}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}} \\ E_{\max} (\text{eV}) &= 0.815 \approx 0.81\text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$

This question relates to **Topic E.2: Quantum physics (HL only)**. Understanding the energy transfer between photons and electrons is key to mastering this topic.

parts.content: Explain why increasing the intensity of the light does not increase the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons.
parts.markscheme:
- Explanation: Intensity determines the number of photons, but the energy of each individual photon depends only on frequency <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define light intensity
steps.content: In the photon model of light, **intensity** is a measure of the number of photons striking a surface per unit area per unit time. When we increase the intensity of the light, we are simply increasing the number of photons arriving every second; we are not changing the properties of the individual photons themselves.

highlights.text: increasing the intensity of the light

steps.title: Energy of individual photons
steps.content:
According to quantum physics, the energy $E$ of a single photon is determined solely by its frequency $f$. This relationship is found in the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet:

$$E = hf$$

Since the frequency of the light remains constant in this scenario, the energy carried by each individual photon remains exactly the same, regardless of how many photons are present (intensity).

steps.title: Photon-electron interaction
steps.content:
The photoelectric effect involves a **one-to-one interaction**: one individual photon transfers all its energy to one individual electron. 

The maximum kinetic energy ($E_{k,\text{max}}$) an electron can have is the energy it receives from a single photon ($hf$) minus the energy required to escape the metal surface (the work function, $\Phi$). 

Because increasing intensity only increases the *quantity* of photons and not the *energy* of each photon, the maximum energy 'kick' an electron can receive remains unchanged.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To summarize for your exam: 
- Intensity determines the **number of photons** arriving per second. 
- The energy of **each individual photon** depends only on frequency ($E=hf$). 
- Since the frequency is constant, the energy per photon is constant, so the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons does not change. 

This concept is a core part of **Topic E.2: Quantum physics**.

steps.title: Define intensity in terms of photons
steps.content: In quantum physics, light is treated as a stream of individual particles called photons. Increasing the **intensity** of the light means that a greater number of photons are hitting the metal surface every second, but it does not change the energy of each individual photon.

steps.title: Analyze individual photon energy
steps.content:
The energy of a single photon depends only on its frequency, as shown in the formula from the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet:

$$E = hf$$

Since the frequency ($f$) of the light remains constant in this scenario, the energy ($E$) delivered by each individual photon to an electron also remains constant, regardless of how many photons (intensity) are present.

steps.title: Apply Einstein's photoelectric equation
steps.content:
Einstein's photoelectric equation relates the energy of the incoming photon to the work function ($\Phi$) and the maximum kinetic energy of the ejected electron:

$$E_{max} = hf - \Phi$$

Because the interaction between a photon and an electron is a **one-to-one** process, the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{max}$) depends only on the energy of that single photon ($hf$) and the energy required to escape the metal surface ($\Phi$).

steps.title: Final justification
steps.content:
Since neither the frequency ($f$) nor the work function ($\Phi$) changes when you increase the intensity, the value of $E_{max}$ remains exactly the same. 

Increasing intensity will result in **more** electrons being emitted per second (a higher photocurrent), but the energy of each individual electron will not increase.

steps.title: Apply one-to-one interaction
steps.content:
In the quantum model of the photoelectric effect, we treat light as a stream of discrete energy packets called **photons**. 

The core principle is the **one-to-one interaction**: one individual photon interacts with, and transfers its energy to, exactly one electron in the metal.

steps.title: Analyze photon energy
steps.content:
The energy of each individual photon depends solely on its frequency. From the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet, we use the equation:

$$E = hf$$

Because the frequency ($f$) of the light has not changed, the energy ($E$) delivered by each individual photon remains exactly the same, regardless of how many photons there are.

steps.title: Define intensity's role
steps.content:
Increasing the **intensity** of the light does not change the energy of the individual photons. Instead, it simply increases the **number of photons** hitting the surface per second. 

While this leads to more interactions and thus more electrons being emitted per second (a higher photocurrent), it does not change the energy available to any single electron.

highlights.text: Intensity determines the number of photons, but the energy of each individual photon depends only on frequency

steps.title: Conclusion on kinetic energy
steps.content:
The maximum kinetic energy ($E_{max}$) of a photoelectron is determined by the energy it receives from a single photon minus the work function ($\Phi$):

$$E_{max} = hf - \Phi$$

Since neither the photon energy ($hf$) nor the work function ($\Phi$) changes when intensity increases, the **maximum kinetic energy remains constant**.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is a common misconception that assumes the stopping potential is directly proportional to the frequency, which neglects the constant work function in Einstein's equation.
Incorrect. This would occur if the work function were added to the photon energy rather than subtracted.

Correct. From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $hf = \Phi + eV$ and $h(2f) = \Phi + eV'$. Rearranging the first equation gives $hf = eV + \Phi$. Substituting this into the second equation: $2(eV + \Phi) = \Phi + eV' \Rightarrow 2eV + 2\Phi = \Phi + eV' \Rightarrow eV' = 2eV + \Phi$. Dividing by $e$ gives $V' = 2V + \frac{\Phi}{e}$. Since the work function $\Phi$ is a positive value, $V'$ must be greater than $2V$.
Incorrect. The stopping potential is dependent on the frequency of the incident light; increasing frequency increases the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons.

parts.content: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the electrons. (Electron mass $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$, charge $e=1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, Planck constant $h=6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$.)
parts.markscheme:
Kinetic energy $E_k = eV = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 150 = 2.40 \times 10^{-17}\ \text{J}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>
-  Momentum $p = \sqrt{2 m_e E_k} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 2.40 \times 10^{-17}} = 6.61 \times 10^{-24}\ \text{kg m s}^{-1}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Wavelength $\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{6.61 \times 10^{-24}} = 1.00 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Calculate kinetic energy
steps.content:
First, we need to find the kinetic energy ($E_k$) the electron gains. When a charge $q$ is accelerated through a potential difference $V$, the work done (which becomes its kinetic energy) is given by $E_k = qV$. Here, the charge is that of an electron, $e$.

$$\begin{aligned} E_k &= e \times V \\ E_k &= (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) \times (150 \text{ V}) \\ E_k &= 2.40 \times 10^{-17} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the kinetic energy in Joules.

highlights.text: accelerated through a potential difference of 150 V

steps.title: Find the electron velocity
steps.content:
Next, we find the velocity ($v$) of the electron using the classic kinetic energy formula from **Topic A.3 (Work, energy and power)**.

From the data booklet:
$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

Rearranging for $v$:
$$\begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{\frac{2E_k}{m}} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2.40 \times 10^{-17} \text{ J}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}}} \\ v &\approx 7.26 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the velocity of the electron.

steps.title: Calculate de Broglie wavelength
steps.content:
Finally, use the de Broglie wavelength formula from **Topic E.2 (Quantum physics)**. This relates the wave properties of a particle to its momentum ($p = mv$).

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv}$$

Substitute the values:
$$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}) \times (7.26 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1})} \\ \lambda &\approx 1.00 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This corresponds to the size of an atom, explaining why electron diffraction is useful for imaging tiny structures!

calculator.description: Calculate the final de Broglie wavelength.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the de Broglie wavelength, we first identify the constants and variables given in the question and data booklet. This problem relates to **Topic E.2: Quantum physics (HL only)**.

- Accelerating potential ($V$): $150\text{ V}$
- Electron mass ($m_e$): $9.11 \times 10^{-31}\text{ kg}$
- Electron charge ($e$): $1.60 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$
- Planck constant ($h$): $6.63 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J s}$

steps.title: Calculate kinetic energy
steps.content:
When an electron is accelerated through a potential difference $V$, the electrical work done on it is converted into kinetic energy ($E_k$). We use the relationship:

$$E_k = qV = eV$$

Substituting the values:
$$E_k = (1.60 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}) \times 150\text{ V}$$
$$E_k = 2.40 \times 10^{-17}\text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the product of charge and voltage.

ti84.instructions: Enter the product of charge and voltage.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the charge of an electron by the accelerating voltage.

calculator.description: Calculate the kinetic energy in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate electron momentum
steps.content:
Next, we find the momentum ($p$) of the electron. We know that kinetic energy is related to momentum by $E_k = \frac{p^2}{2m}$. Rearranging for momentum gives:

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e E_k}$$

Substituting our values:
$$p = \sqrt{2 \times (9.11 \times 10^{-31}\text{ kg}) \times (2.40 \times 10^{-17}\text{ J})}$$
$$p \approx 6.61 \times 10^{-24}\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the square root of the product 2 * mass * energy.

ti84.instructions: Use the square root of the product 2 * mass * energy.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the square root of 2 times the mass of an electron times the kinetic energy calculated in the previous step.

calculator.description: Calculate momentum from kinetic energy and mass.

steps.title: Calculate de Broglie wavelength
steps.content:
Finally, we use the de Broglie relation to find the wavelength ($\lambda$):

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

Substituting the Planck constant and the momentum we just calculated:
$$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J s}}{6.61 \times 10^{-24}\text{ kg m s}^{-1}}$$
$$\lambda = 1.00 \times 10^{-10}\text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide h by the previous result (Ans).

desmos.instructions: Divide the Planck constant by the momentum calculated in the previous step.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength by dividing Planck's constant by momentum.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The de Broglie wavelength of the electrons is:

$$\lambda = 1.00 \times 10^{-10}\text{ m}$$

This result is consistent with the size of atoms (approximately $10^{-10}\text{ m}$), which explains why electrons can be used to study atomic structures through diffraction.

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, we list the values provided in the question and the fundamental constants needed for the calculation:

*   Potential difference: $V = 150\text{ V}$
*   Mass of electron: $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\text{ kg}$
*   Elementary charge: $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$
*   Planck constant: $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J s}$

steps.title: Combine the physical equations
steps.content:
To find the wavelength in one step, we combine three fundamental concepts:
1.  **Kinetic Energy ($E_k$):** When an electron is accelerated, its energy is $E_k = eV$.
2.  **Momentum ($p$):** From Mechanics, we know $E_k = \frac{p^2}{2m}$, which rearranges to $p = \sqrt{2m E_k}$. Substituting $E_k$, we get $p = \sqrt{2meV}$.
3.  **De Broglie Wavelength:** The wavelength is given by $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$.

By substituting the momentum expression into the wavelength formula, we get our single consolidated expression:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength
steps.content:
Now, we substitute all the known values into our consolidated formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times (9.11 \times 10^{-31}) \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19}) \times 150}}$$

Using a calculator for the denominator first:
$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 150} \approx 6.6127 \times 10^{-24}\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$$

Then completing the division:
$$\lambda \approx 1.0026 \times 10^{-10}\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Input the fraction to ensure the entire root is in the denominator.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression carefully using parentheses for the denominator and square root. Use [EE] for scientific notation.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula as a single fraction to visualize the calculation clearly.

calculator.description: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength directly using the consolidated formula.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
Rounding our result to three significant figures (matching the given data), we get:

$$\lambda = 1.00 \times 10^{-10}\text{ m}$$

**Marks Distribution:**
*   **1 mark:** Correct calculation of kinetic energy ($eV$) or correct formula setup.
*   **1 mark:** Correct calculation of momentum ($p$) or substitution into the combined root.
*   **1 mark:** Final correct answer with units.

parts.content: Explain why the observation of diffraction patterns from electrons demonstrates wave–particle duality.
parts.markscheme:
- Electrons behave as particles but produce interference patterns typical of waves <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- This demonstrates wave–particle duality, a fundamental quantum concept <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the nature of electrons
steps.content: First, we must establish what an electron is in classical physics. Electrons are fundamental subatomic particles that possess mass and electric charge. They are constituents of **matter**.

steps.title: Identify the nature of diffraction
steps.content: Next, we look at the phenomenon mentioned in the question: diffraction. Diffraction is the spreading of a wave as it passes through an aperture or around an obstacle. It is a property that is **exclusive to waves**; classical particles do not diffract.

steps.title: Connect particle and wave behaviors
steps.content:
This leads to a contradiction in classical physics: an object we know to be a particle (the electron) is exhibiting a behavior defined for waves (diffraction). 

The fact that matter (electrons) produces interference patterns typical of waves leads to the conclusion that electrons exhibit properties of **both** particles and waves. This concept is known as **wave–particle duality**.

steps.title: State the final explanation
steps.content:
To secure the full marks, you should structure your answer to explicitly contrast the particle nature with the observed wave behavior:

1.  **Electrons are particles** (they have mass and charge).
2.  **Diffraction is a wave property** (interference patterns).
3.  Therefore, electrons behaving like waves demonstrates **wave–particle duality**.

parts.content: Describe how increasing the accelerating voltage affects the diffraction pattern.
parts.markscheme: Increasing accelerating voltage increases electron momentum, decreasing de Broglie wavelength and narrowing the diffraction pattern <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Analyze the effect on momentum
steps.content:
First, we look at how the accelerating voltage affects the electrons. The electric field does work on the electrons, increasing their kinetic energy ($E_k$) according to the relationship $E_k = qV$.

Increasing the **accelerating voltage** increases the kinetic energy. Since momentum $p$ is related to kinetic energy (by $p = \sqrt{2mE_k}$), an increase in voltage leads to an increase in the momentum of the electrons.

steps.title: Determine change in wavelength
steps.content:
Next, we relate the momentum to the de Broglie wavelength using the formula:

$$ \lambda = \frac{h}{p} $$

Since the momentum $p$ has **increased**, and Planck's constant $h$ is fixed, the de Broglie wavelength $\lambda$ **decreases**.

steps.title: Link wavelength to diffraction
steps.content:
Finally, we connect the wavelength to the appearance of the diffraction pattern. The amount of diffraction (spread of the pattern) is directly proportional to the wavelength $\lambda$. For a single slit, the angle $\theta$ to the first minimum is approximately:

$$ \theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{b} $$

Because the wavelength $\lambda$ has decreased, the angle of diffraction $\theta$ decreases. This results in the diffraction pattern becoming **narrower** (or the rings/fringes becoming closer together).

steps.title: Relate voltage to velocity
steps.content:
First, we relate the accelerating voltage to the electron's speed. The work done by the voltage ($eV$) converts to the electron's kinetic energy ($E_k$). Using the kinetic energy formula from the **Mechanics** section:

$$eV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Rearranging for velocity $v$ gives:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}}$$

Note: We take the square root because $v$ is squared in the energy formula.

steps.title: Link velocity to wavelength
steps.content:
Next, we use the de Broglie wavelength formula $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. We substitute the expression for velocity we just found:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{m \left( \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}} \right)}$$

Simplifying this expression shows that wavelength is inversely proportional to the square root of the voltage:

$$\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{V}}$$

This derived relationship allows us to predict how the wavelength responds to voltage changes.

steps.title: Determine the effect on wavelength
steps.content:
Now we apply the change described in the question. As the accelerating voltage **increases**, the term $\sqrt{V}$ increases.

Because of the inverse relationship $\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{V}}$, the de Broglie wavelength $\lambda$ **decreases**.

steps.title: Describe the diffraction pattern change
steps.content:
Finally, we link the wavelength to the diffraction pattern. The angle of diffraction $\theta$ is determined by the ratio of wavelength to slit width:

$$\sin\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{b}$$

Since the wavelength $\lambda$ has **decreased**, the diffraction angle $\theta$ also decreases. This means the diffraction pattern becomes **narrower** (the fringes move closer together).

This value incorrectly accounts for the kinetic energy of only one of the two particles produced in the interaction.

This is the total energy (rest mass energy plus kinetic energy) of a single particle, rather than the pair produced by the photon.

parts.content: State the equation for the change in wavelength due to Compton scattering.
parts.markscheme:
- State the equation: $\Delta \lambda = \frac{h}{m_ec}(1 - \cos\theta)$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: The question asks for the specific mathematical equation that describes the **Compton shift**, which is the change in wavelength of a photon when it scatters off a particle (like an electron).

steps.title: State the equation
steps.content:
The standard equation for the change in wavelength $\Delta \lambda$ due to Compton scattering is:

$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{h}{m_ec}(1 - \cos\theta)$$

Where:
*   $h$ is Planck's constant
*   $m_e$ is the mass of the electron
*   $c$ is the speed of light
*   $\theta$ is the angle at which the photon is scattered

parts.content: Calculate the wavelength shift when the scattering angle is $90^\circ$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the formula: $\Delta\lambda = \frac{6.63\times10^{-34}}{9.11\times10^{-31}\times3\times10^8}(1-\cos90^\circ)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the final answer: $\Delta\lambda = 2.43\times10^{-12}\ \text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award M1 for correct substitution into formula, A1 for correct numerical value.</note>

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the wavelength shift, we use the Compton scattering formula found in **Topic E.2 (Quantum Physics)**:

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos\theta)$$

Where:
- $\Delta\lambda$ is the wavelength shift
- $h$ is Planck's constant
- $m_e$ is the mass of the electron
- $c$ is the speed of light
- $\theta$ is the scattering angle

steps.title: Identify the known values
steps.content:
We identify the values for the constants from the data booklet and the angle from the question:

*   **Angle:** $\theta = 90^\circ$
*   **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$
*   **Speed of light:** $c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}$
*   **Electron mass:** $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$ (standard constant)

steps.title: Substitute values
steps.content:
Substitute these values into the equation:

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{6.63\times10^{-34}}{(9.11\times10^{-31})(3.00\times10^8)}(1 - \cos 90^\circ)$$

Since $\cos 90^\circ = 0$, the term $(1 - \cos 90^\circ)$ simplifies to $1$.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient h / (me * c)

steps.title: Calculate the final answer
steps.content:
Now, compute the numerical value:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta\lambda &= \frac{6.63\times10^{-34}}{2.733\times10^{-22}} \\ \Delta\lambda &\approx 2.43 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Explain why this phenomenon supports the particle nature of light and its relevance in medical imaging.
parts.markscheme:
- Support for particle nature: Compton scattering involves the transfer of energy/momentum between photons (light particles) and electrons. This can only be explained by treating light as particles (photons) rather than waves <marks>A1</marks>

- Relevance in medical imaging: Compton scattering causes X-rays to be scattered in different directions inside the body. This scattering reduces image contrast/quality. Understanding Compton scattering helps develop techniques to minimize its effects and improve image quality <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>No marks for just stating "wave-particle duality" without explanation</note>

steps.title: Analyze the interaction type
steps.content:
First, let's look at what is happening in the experiment. Compton scattering describes an interaction where an incident X-ray interacts with an electron. Instead of acting like a continuous wave washing over the electron, the X-ray behaves like a discrete object colliding with it. 

This is best understood by imagining a game of billiards (pool), where a moving ball (the X-ray) hits a stationary ball (the electron).

steps.title: Argument for particle nature
steps.content:
To explain the results of this experiment, we must treat the X-ray as a stream of particles called **photons**. 

During the collision, there is a transfer of **momentum** and energy from the photon to the electron. This follows the laws of conservation of momentum and energy, just like solid particles colliding. A pure wave model cannot easily explain this specific exchange of momentum that results in the scattered X-ray having a longer wavelength (lower energy). This provides strong evidence for the **particle nature of light**.

steps.title: Impact on medical imaging
steps.content:
Now, let's apply this to medical imaging. For a clear X-ray image, we want the X-ray photons to travel in straight lines from the source, through the patient, to the detector. Shadows created by bones absorbing these rays form the image.

However, Compton scattering causes some X-rays to bounce off electrons and change direction (scatter) while passing through the body.

steps.title: Effect on image contrast
steps.content:
These scattered X-rays can hit the detector at random locations, rather than where they were originally aimed. This creates a general "fog" or background noise on the image, which **degrades the image contrast**.

Instead of a sharp black-and-white image, the distinction between different tissues becomes blurry or gray. Understanding this helps engineers design grids and filters to block scattered rays and improve image quality.

parts.content: Calculate the energy of the incident photons in electronvolts.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute into equation for photon energy: $E=\frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)\left(3.00 \times 10^8\right)}{200 \times 10^{-9}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate energy in Joules: $E=9.95 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{\ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Convert to electronvolts: $E=\frac{9.95 \times 10^{-19}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}}=6.22 \mathrm{\ eV}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the combined formula
steps.content:
To find the energy of a photon from its wavelength, we combine two fundamental equations from the data booklet:
1. The photon energy equation: $$E = hf$$
2. The wave equation: $$c = f\lambda$$

By rearranging the wave equation to $f = \frac{c}{\lambda}$ and substituting it into the energy equation, we get the combined formula:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate Joules
steps.content:
We'll substitute the given values into the formula. Remember to convert the wavelength from nanometers to meters: $$200 \text{ nm} = 200 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} E &= \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1})}{200 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} \\ E &= 9.945 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, we round this intermediate value to $$9.95 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find energy in Joules.

calculator.description: Calculate the energy in Joules using the combined formula.

steps.title: Convert to electronvolts
steps.content:
Now, we convert the energy from Joules to electronvolts (eV) by dividing by the elementary charge ($e$), which is found in the Physical Constants section of your data booklet.

$$\begin{aligned} E (\text{eV}) &= \frac{E (\text{J})}{e} \\ E (\text{eV}) &= \frac{9.945 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}} \\ E &= 6.2156... \text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get **6.22 eV**.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the energy of the incident photons, we first identify the given information from the question. We are provided with the wavelength ($\lambda$) of the light:

$$\lambda = 200 \text{ nm} = 200 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

This topic is part of **Topic E: Nuclear and quantum physics**. To solve this using the two-step frequency approach, we will first find the frequency of the light and then use it to find the energy.

steps.title: Calculate the frequency
steps.content:
Using the wave equation found in the **Waves** section of your data booklet, we can solve for frequency ($f$). The speed of light ($c$) is a constant $3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

$$\begin{aligned} v &= f\lambda \\ c &= f\lambda \\ f &= \frac{c}{\lambda} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the values:

$$f = \frac{3.00 \times 10^{8}}{200 \times 10^{-9}}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the speed of light by the wavelength.

desmos.instructions: Divide the speed of light by the wavelength.

steps.title: State the frequency
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives us the frequency in Hertz (Hz):

$$f = 1.50 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}$$

steps.title: Calculate energy in Joules
steps.content:
Now we use the Planck equation from the **Nuclear** section of the data booklet to find the energy ($E$) in Joules. We use Planck's constant $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$.

$$E = hf$$

Substituting our calculated frequency:

$$E = (6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (1.50 \times 10^{15})$$

$$E = 9.945 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Convert energy to electronvolts
steps.content:
To convert from Joules to electronvolts (eV), we divide the energy by the elementary charge $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, as $1 \text{ eV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

$$E_{\text{eV}} = \frac{E_{\text{Joules}}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$E_{\text{eV}} = \frac{9.945 \times 10^{-19}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}}$$
