steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures, we find the energy of the incident photons:

$$E = 6.22 \text{ eV}$$

In an IB exam, you earn marks for correctly substituting into the energy equations (M1), finding the value in Joules (A1), and correctly converting to eV (A1).

parts.content: Determine whether photoelectrons are emitted from the metal surface. Justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- Compare photon energy to work function: The photon energy $6.22 \mathrm{\ eV}$ is greater than the work function $3.5 \mathrm{\ eV}$ <marks>R1</marks>

- Conclude that emission occurs: Therefore, photoelectrons are emitted <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

methods.label: Comparing Photon Energy to Work Function

steps.title: Identify the emission condition
steps.content:
For photoelectrons to be emitted from a metal surface, the energy of an incident photon ($E$) must be greater than or equal to the work function ($\Phi$) of the metal.

$$E \geq \Phi$$

This concept is a key part of **Topic E.1: Structure of the atom** and Einstein's explanation of the photoelectric effect.

steps.title: Calculate photon energy
steps.content:
First, we calculate the energy of the incident ultraviolet light photons in Joules. We combine the equations $$E = hf$$ and $$v = f\lambda$$ (where $v=c$) from the data booklet to get:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Substituting the given values:
- $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$
- $c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}$
- $\lambda = 200 \text{ nm} = 200 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$

$$E = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^{8}}{200 \times 10^{-9}}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the basic division and multiplication functions.

ti84.instructions: Enter the values into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the photon energy in Joules.

steps.title: Convert energy to electronvolts
steps.content:
Since the work function is provided in electronvolts ($eV$), we must convert our photon energy from Joules to $eV$ by dividing by the elementary charge ($e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$).

$$E_{\text{eV}} = \frac{9.945 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}}$$

$$E_{\text{eV}} \approx 6.22 \text{ eV}$$

desmos.instructions: Perform the division in a new cell.

calculator.description: Divide the Joules result by the elementary charge to get eV.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
Now we compare the photon energy ($E \approx 6.22 \text{ eV}$) to the work function ($\Phi = 3.5 \text{ eV}$).

Since **$6.22 \text{ eV} > 3.5 \text{ eV}$**, the energy of a single photon is sufficient to liberate an electron from the metal surface.

Therefore, **photoelectrons are emitted**.

steps.title: Define emission condition
steps.content:
To determine if photoelectrons are emitted, we compare the wavelength of the incident light ($\lambda$) to the **threshold wavelength** ($\lambda_0$). 

Photoelectrons are emitted only if the incident wavelength is **shorter than or equal to** the threshold wavelength:
$$\lambda \le \lambda_0$$

This is because shorter wavelengths correspond to higher energy photons. If the wavelength is short enough, the photon carries enough energy to overcome the metal's work function.

steps.title: Calculate threshold wavelength
steps.content:
The threshold wavelength $\lambda_0$ is the maximum wavelength that can cause emission. It is related to the work function $\Phi$ by combining the equations $E = hf$ and $c = f\lambda$:

$$\Phi = \frac{hc}{\lambda_0} \implies \lambda_0 = \frac{hc}{\Phi}$$

First, convert the work function from electron-volts (eV) to Joules (J) using the elementary charge $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ from the data booklet:
$$\Phi = 3.5 \text{ eV} \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}) = 5.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

Now, substitute the constants $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ and $c = 3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$:
$$\lambda_0 = \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3.00 \times 10^8)}{5.60 \times 10^{-19}}$$
$$\lambda_0 \approx 3.55 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 355 \text{ nm}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the expression using constants for h, c, and e.

calculator.description: Calculate the threshold wavelength in meters.

steps.title: Compare wavelengths and conclude
steps.content:
Now we compare the incident wavelength $\lambda$ to the threshold wavelength $\lambda_0$:

- Incident wavelength: $\lambda = 200 \text{ nm}$
- Threshold wavelength: $\lambda_0 \approx 355 \text{ nm}$

Because **$\lambda < \lambda_0$**, the incident light has a shorter wavelength (and therefore higher energy) than the minimum required to eject an electron.

**Conclusion:** Photoelectrons **are emitted** from the metal surface.

steps.title: Calculate incident light frequency
steps.content:
To determine if photoelectrons are emitted, we first find the frequency $f$ of the incident ultraviolet light. We use the wave equation from the data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Rearranging for frequency $f$ and using the speed of light $c$ for $v$:

$$f = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\begin{aligned} f &= \frac{3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}}{200 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} \\ f &= 1.50 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the division of the constants.

ti84.instructions: Divide the speed of light by the wavelength in meters.

calculator.description: Calculate the frequency of the incident light.

steps.title: Calculate the threshold frequency
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the threshold frequency $f_0$, which is the minimum frequency required to eject an electron. This is related to the work function $\Phi$ by the equation:

$$\Phi = hf_0 \implies f_0 = \frac{\Phi}{h}$$

First, convert the work function from electronvolts (eV) to Joules (J) using the elementary charge $e$ from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} \Phi &= 3.5 \text{ eV} \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}) \\ \Phi &= 5.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Now, solve for $f_0$:

$$\begin{aligned} f_0 &= \frac{5.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}} \\ f_0 &\approx 8.45 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the work function (in Joules) by Planck's constant.

ti84.instructions: Multiply work function by e, then divide by h.

calculator.description: Calculate the threshold frequency.

steps.title: Compare frequencies and conclude
steps.content:
For photoelectrons to be emitted, the frequency of the incident light must be greater than the threshold frequency ($f > f_0$).

Comparing our results:
- Incident frequency $f = 1.50 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}$
- Threshold frequency $f_0 = 0.845 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}$

Since $1.50 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz} > 8.45 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$, the condition is met.

**Conclusion**: Photoelectrons are emitted from the metal surface because the incident light frequency exceeds the threshold frequency.

parts.content: If photoelectrons are emitted, calculate their maximum kinetic energy in joules.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate maximum kinetic energy in eV: $E_{\max } = 6.22 - 3.5 = 2.72 \mathrm{\ eV}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Convert result to Joules: $E_{\max } = 2.72 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} = 4.35 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{\ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

methods.label: Work in eV then convert to Joules

steps.title: Calculate photon energy (Joules)
steps.content:
First, we need to find the energy of the incoming ultraviolet photons. We can use the relationship between energy and wavelength from the data booklet. 

By combining $$E = hf$$ and $$v = f\lambda$$ (where $v = c$), we get:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Using the values from the physical constants:
- $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$
- $c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}$
- $\lambda = 200 \text{ nm} = 200 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$

$$E = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^{8}}{200 \times 10^{-9}} = 9.945 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the photon energy in Joules.

steps.title: Convert photon energy to eV
steps.content:
Since the work function is given in electron-volts (eV), it is easier to work in those units. We convert the photon energy from Joules to eV using the elementary charge $e$ as the conversion factor.

$$E_{\text{eV}} = \frac{E_{\text{Joules}}}{e} = \frac{9.945 \times 10^{-19}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$E_{\text{eV}} = 6.215625 \approx 6.22 \text{ eV}$$

calculator.description: Convert Joules to eV.

steps.title: Calculate kinetic energy (eV)
steps.content:
Now we apply Einstein's photoelectric equation to find the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{\text{max}}$) of the emitted electrons. The energy of the photon minus the energy needed to escape the metal (the work function $\Phi$) equals the kinetic energy leftover.

$$E_{\text{max}} = E_{\text{photon}} - \Phi$$

$$E_{\text{max}} = 6.22 \text{ eV} - 3.5 \text{ eV} = 2.72 \text{ eV}$$

This calculation earns you the first mark (**M1**).

highlights.text: Calculate maximum kinetic energy in eV: $E_{\max } = 6.22 - 3.5 = 2.72 \mathrm{\ eV}$

steps.title: Convert result to Joules
steps.content:
The question asks for the final answer in **joules**. We convert our result back by multiplying the energy in eV by the conversion factor $1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}$.

$$E_{\text{max}} = 2.72 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$E_{\text{max}} = 4.35 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

This final conversion earns you the second mark (**A1**).

calculator.description: Convert the final result from eV to Joules.

methods.label: Work in Joules from the start

steps.title: Convert work function to Joules
steps.content:
To solve this problem by working in Joules from the start, we first convert the work function $\Phi$ from electron-volts (eV) to Joules (J). We use the elementary charge $e$ from the data booklet as the conversion factor.

$$\Phi = 3.5 \text{ eV} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}$$

$$\Phi = 5.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the work function in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate photon energy in Joules
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the energy of the incident photons. Since we are given the wavelength $\lambda$, we combine the wave equation $c = f\lambda$ and the energy equation $E = hf$ to get:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Substituting the values for Planck's constant ($h$), the speed of light ($c$), and the wavelength ($200 \text{ nm}$):

$$E = \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s})(3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1})}{200 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

$$E = 9.945 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the photon energy in Joules.

steps.title: Determine maximum kinetic energy
steps.content:
Einstein's photoelectric equation relates the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{k, \text{max}}$) of the emitted electrons to the photon energy ($E$) and the work function ($\Phi$):

$$E_{k, \text{max}} = E - \Phi$$

Subtract the values we calculated in Joules:

$$E_{k, \text{max}} = 9.945 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} - 5.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

$$E_{k, \text{max}} = 4.345 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final kinetic energy.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures, as is standard in IB Physics, we get:

$$E_{k, \text{max}} = 4.35 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

This calculation would earn 2 marks: one for the method of determining the energies in a consistent unit system and one for the correct final value in Joules.

options.markscheme: This assumes that the wavelength is directly proportional to the potential difference ($$\lambda \propto V$$), which is incorrect.
This assumes that the wavelength is directly proportional to the square root of the potential difference ($$\lambda \propto \sqrt{V}$$), which is incorrect.

The de Broglie wavelength is given by $$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$. Since the kinetic energy is $$E_k = qV = \frac{p^2}{2m}$$, it follows that $$p = \sqrt{2mqV}$$. Therefore, $$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mqV}}$$, indicating that $$\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{V}}$$. If the potential difference increases by a factor of 4, the wavelength decreases by a factor of $$\sqrt{4} = 2$$.

This assumes that the wavelength is inversely proportional to the potential difference ($$\lambda \propto \frac{1}{V}$$), failing to account for the square root relationship between momentum and kinetic energy.

questions.specification: In a Compton scattering experiment, an X-ray photon of initial wavelength $\lambda_i = 0.050\ \text{nm}$ collides with a stationary electron. The photon is scattered at an angle of $60^\circ$.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: E.2.1 Wave–particle duality (HL only)

parts.content: State the Compton wavelength shift formula.
parts.markscheme: $\Delta \lambda = \lambda_f - \lambda_i = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos \theta)$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the physics concept
steps.content: In this problem, we are looking at **Compton scattering**, a phenomenon studied in **Topic E.2: Quantum Physics (HL)**. This effect provides evidence for the particle nature of light by showing that photons carry momentum and can collide with electrons, losing energy and increasing in wavelength.

steps.title: State the Compton formula
steps.content:
The standard equation used to calculate the change in wavelength ($\Delta \lambda$) for a photon scattered by a stationary electron is given by:

$$\Delta \lambda = \lambda_f - \lambda_i = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos \theta)$$

This equation directly relates the increase in the photon's wavelength to the scattering angle $\theta$.

steps.title: Define the formula variables
steps.content:
To ensure full marks, it is important to understand what each term in the formula represents:

- $\Delta \lambda$: The Compton wavelength shift.
- $\lambda_f$: The wavelength of the scattered photon.
- $\lambda_i$: The initial wavelength of the incident photon.
- $h$: Planck’s constant ($6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$).
- $m_e$: The rest mass of the electron ($9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$).
- $c$: The speed of light in a vacuum ($3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$).
- $\theta$: The angle at which the photon is scattered.

parts.content: Calculate the change in wavelength $\Delta \lambda$ of the photon. Use $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$, and $c = 3.00 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m s}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
- $\frac{h}{m_e c} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.00 \times 10^{8}} = 2.43 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$
- $\Delta \lambda = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} (1 - \cos 60^\circ) = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} (1 - 0.5) = 1.22 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m} = 0.00122\ \text{nm}$ 
- Correct substitution <marks> 1 mark </marks> and value <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the Compton shift formula
steps.content:
To find the change in wavelength $\Delta \lambda$, we use the **Compton scattering formula**. This formula relates the change in the photon's wavelength to the scattering angle $\theta$ and physical constants:

$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos \theta)$$

Where:
- $h$ is Planck's constant
- $m_e$ is the rest mass of the electron
- $c$ is the speed of light
- $\theta$ is the scattering angle of the photon

steps.title: Calculate the constant factor
steps.content:
First, let's calculate the value of the term $\frac{h}{m_e c}$, which is known as the Compton wavelength of the electron. Substituting the values provided in the question:

$$\frac{h}{m_e c} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg})(3.00 \times 10^8\ \text{m s}^{-1})}$$

Evaluating this:

$$\frac{h}{m_e c} \approx 2.43 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the division with scientific notation.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression using parentheses for the denominator.

calculator.description: Calculate the Compton wavelength factor.

steps.title: Calculate the angular factor
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the term $(1 - \cos \theta)$ using the scattering angle $\theta = 60^\circ$. 

Recall that $\cos(60^\circ) = 0.5$, so:

$$(1 - \cos 60^\circ) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

gdc.instructions: Check degree mode and calculate the expression.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode, then press 1 - cos(60).

desmos.instructions: Switch to degrees in settings and evaluate.

calculator.description: Verify the value of the cosine term.

steps.title: Determine the change in wavelength
steps.content:
Finally, multiply the two results together to find $\Delta \lambda$:

$$\Delta \lambda = (2.43 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}) \times 0.5$$
$$\Delta \lambda = 1.215 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision of the constants provided), we get:

$$\Delta \lambda = 1.22 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$$

To convert this to nanometers ($1\ \text{nm} = 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$):

$$\Delta \lambda = 0.00122\ \text{nm}$$

parts.content: Determine the wavelength $\lambda_f$ of the scattered photon.
parts.markscheme: $\lambda_f = \lambda_i + \Delta \lambda = 0.050 + 0.00122 = 0.05122\ \text{nm}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify Compton scattering variables
steps.content:
To find the final wavelength, we use the Compton scattering formula, which relates the change in wavelength ($\Delta \lambda$) to the scattering angle ($\theta$). 

From the question, we have:
- Initial wavelength: $\lambda_i = 0.050\ \text{nm}$
- Scattering angle: $\theta = 60^\circ$
- Electron mass ($m_e$): $9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength shift
steps.content:
The Compton shift formula is given by:
$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos\theta)$$

Using the values from the data booklet:
- $$h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$$
- $$c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

Substituting the values:
$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(3.00 \times 10^8)}(1 - \cos 60^\circ)$$

Since $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$, the shift is:
$$\Delta \lambda \approx 1.21 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m} = 0.00122\ \text{nm}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the constants and use the cosine function in degrees.

ti84.instructions: Enter the formula components directly. Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode.

calculator.description: Calculate the Compton wavelength shift in meters.

steps.title: Find the final wavelength
steps.content:
The final wavelength ($\lambda_f$) is the sum of the initial wavelength and the shift calculated in the previous step:

$$\lambda_f = \lambda_i + \Delta \lambda$$

Substituting the values in nm:
$$\lambda_f = 0.050 + 0.00122$$
$$\lambda_f = 0.05122\ \text{nm}$$

parts.content: Explain how Compton scattering supports the particle nature of light.
parts.markscheme: The increase in wavelength indicates photons carry momentum and behave like particles in collisions with electrons <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Treat light as a particle
steps.content: In the particle collision model, we treat the interaction between the X-ray and the electron as a discrete collision between two particles, much like billiard balls. This approach relies on the conservation of momentum and energy, which are core concepts from **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Analyze the wavelength shift
steps.content:
In the experiment, the scattered photon is found to have a longer wavelength than the incident photon. According to the energy-frequency relationship:

$$E = hf = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

An increase in wavelength ($\lambda$) means the photon has lost energy. This energy was transferred to the stationary electron to give it kinetic energy, causing it to recoil.

steps.title: Link to particle behavior
steps.content: Classical wave theory predicts that the frequency (and thus wavelength) of light should not change during scattering. However, the observed increase in wavelength proves that photons carry momentum and exchange it during collisions, which is a defining characteristic of particles.

highlights.text: The increase in wavelength indicates photons carry momentum and behave like particles in collisions with electrons

steps.title: Relate energy to wavelength
steps.content:
To understand why this experiment supports the particle nature of light, we first look at how the energy of a photon is defined. In the quantum model, light consists of discrete packets called photons. Using the equations for energy and wave speed from your data booklet, we can relate energy ($E$) to wavelength ($\lambda$):

$$\begin{aligned} E &= hf \\ c &= f\lambda \implies f = \frac{c}{\lambda} \\ E &= \frac{hc}{\lambda} \end{aligned}$$

This shows that the energy of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength.

steps.title: Analyze the collision
steps.content:
In Compton scattering, we treat the interaction like a billiard-ball collision between two particles: a photon and an electron. When the incoming photon hits the stationary electron, it transfers some of its kinetic energy to the electron. 

Since the photon has given away some energy, the scattered photon must have **less energy** than the initial photon.

steps.title: Connect energy loss to wavelength
steps.content:
Because energy $E$ and wavelength $\lambda$ are inversely proportional ($E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}$), a decrease in the photon's energy results in an **increase in its wavelength**. 

This shift in wavelength is a direct result of light behaving as a discrete particle that can lose a specific amount of energy during a collision. Classical wave theory cannot explain this, as it would predict the energy depends on the wave's amplitude, not its wavelength.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: The observation that the wavelength increases ($ \lambda_{f} > \lambda_{i} $) confirms that photons carry discrete amounts of energy and momentum, behaving as particles during collisions with electrons.

steps.title: Classical wave prediction
steps.content:
According to **classical wave theory**, light is an electromagnetic wave. When an electromagnetic wave hits a stationary electron, the electric field should cause the electron to oscillate at the **same frequency** as the incident light.

Under this classical model, the scattered light should have the exact same frequency and wavelength as the original beam. There is no mechanism in classical physics for the wavelength to change just by hitting a single electron.

steps.title: The observed wavelength shift
steps.content:
In the experiment, however, we observe that the scattered X-rays have a **longer wavelength** than the incident X-rays ($\lambda_f > \lambda_i$). 

Using the wave relationship from the data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Since the speed of light $c$ is constant, an increase in wavelength $\lambda$ corresponds to a decrease in frequency $f$. This directly contradicts the classical prediction that the frequency should remain unchanged.

steps.title: Particle-like collisions
steps.content:
To explain this shift, we must treat light as a stream of discrete particles called **photons**. 

When a photon collides with an electron, it behaves like a billiard ball in a mechanics problem (see **Topic A.2.3: Collisions**). During the collision, the photon transfers some of its energy and momentum to the electron. Because the photon loses energy, its frequency must decrease according to the equation:

$$E = hf$$

As the frequency $f$ decreases, the wavelength $\lambda$ must increase. This shift is only possible if light carries discrete packets of energy and momentum.

steps.title: Supporting the particle nature
steps.content: The fact that the wavelength increases indicates that energy was transferred in a quantized, one-on-one interaction. This demonstrates that light has **momentum** and behaves like a **particle** during collisions, providing clear evidence for the particle nature of light that classical wave theory cannot explain.

parts.content: Calculate the initial wavelength of the photon and the shift in wavelength after the collision.
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** energy in Joules: $E = 80 \times 10^3 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} = 1.28 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{J}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** initial wavelength: $\lambda_i = \dfrac{hc}{E} = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}{1.28 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.55 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Substitute** into Compton shift formula: $\Delta\lambda = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}(1 - \cos 60^\circ)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** shift in wavelength: $\Delta\lambda = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} \times (0.5) = 1.21 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Convert energy to Joules
steps.content:
To perform calculations in SI units, we first need to convert the photon's energy from kiloelectron-volts (keV) to Joules (J).

1.  **Convert keV to eV**: 
    $$80 \text{ keV} = 80 \times 10^3 \text{ eV}$$
2.  **Convert eV to Joules**: Use the elementary charge constant $e$ from the data booklet:
    $$\begin{aligned} E &= 80 \times 10^3 \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19}) \\ E &= 1.28 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This conversion is a fundamental skill in **Topic E: Nuclear and quantum physics**.

calculator.description: Calculate the energy in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate initial wavelength
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the initial wavelength ($\lambda_i$) using the energy-wavelength relationship derived from $E = hf$ and $v = f\lambda$.

Combining these formulas gives:
$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \implies \lambda_i = \frac{hc}{E}$$

Substituting the known values:
$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_i &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}{1.28 \times 10^{-14}} \\ \lambda_i &= 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the initial wavelength.

steps.title: Determine the wavelength shift
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the shift in wavelength ($\Delta\lambda$) using the Compton scattering formula:

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos\theta)$$

Where:
- $m_e$ is the mass of the electron ($9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$)
- $\theta$ is the scattering angle of $60^\circ$

Substituting the values:
$$\begin{aligned} \Delta\lambda &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}(1 - \cos 60^\circ) \\ \Delta\lambda &= 2.43 \times 10^{-12} \times (1 - 0.5) \\ \Delta\lambda &= 1.21 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter (6.63E-34 / (9.11E-31 * 3E8)) * (1 - cos(60)). Ensure mode is set to Degrees.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength shift.

steps.title: Convert energy to Joules
steps.content:
First, we need to convert the photon's energy from kiloelectron-volts (keV) to the standard SI unit, Joules (J), so we can use it in our momentum formulas. 

Given:
$$E = 80 \text{ keV} = 80,000 \text{ eV}$$

Using the elementary charge $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ from the data booklet:
$$\begin{aligned} E &= 80,000 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \\ E &= 1.28 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This conversion is essential for accuracy in later steps.

calculator.description: Convert energy to Joules.

steps.title: Determine initial momentum
steps.content:
In the momentum-based approach, we treat the photon as a particle. For a photon, the relationship between energy ($E$) and momentum ($p$) is given by:

$$p = \frac{E}{c}$$

Using the speed of light $c = 3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ from the data booklet:
$$\begin{aligned} p &= \frac{1.28 \times 10^{-14}}{3.00 \times 10^8} \\ p &= 4.27 \times 10^{-23} \text{ kg m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate initial momentum.

steps.title: Calculate initial wavelength
steps.content:
Now, we apply the de Broglie relationship to find the initial wavelength ($\lambda_i$). This concept, found in **Topic E.2: Quantum Physics**, relates a particle's momentum to its wavelength.

$$ \lambda_i = \frac{h}{p} $$

Using Planck's constant $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$:
$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_i &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{4.266... \times 10^{-23}} \\ \lambda_i &= 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This is the initial wavelength of the X-ray photon.

calculator.description: Calculate wavelength from momentum.

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength shift
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the shift in wavelength ($\Delta \lambda$) using the Compton scattering formula. The photon scatters at an angle of $60^\circ$.

$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos \theta)$$

Where $m_e$ is the mass of the electron ($9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$):
$$\begin{aligned} \Delta \lambda &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}(1 - \cos 60^\circ) \\ \Delta \lambda &= (2.43 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1 - 0.5) \\ \Delta \lambda &= 1.21 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Note that $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$, which simplifies the calculation significantly.

calculator.description: Calculate wavelength shift.

steps.title: Identify Energy and Shortcut
steps.content:
To find the initial wavelength, we first identify the photon's energy in electron-volts (eV). The question provides the energy in kiloelectron-volts (keV). We will use the **eV-nm shortcut** for the product of Planck's constant ($h$) and the speed of light ($c$):

$$hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}$$

Given energy:
$$E = 80 \text{ keV} = 80,000 \text{ eV}$$

steps.title: Calculate Initial Wavelength
steps.content:
Using the relationship $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$, we can solve for the initial wavelength ($\lambda_i$) directly in nanometers (nm) using our shortcut:

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_i &= \frac{hc}{E} \\ \lambda_i &= \frac{1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}}{80,000 \text{ eV}} \\ \lambda_i &= 0.0155 \text{ nm} \end{aligned}$$

Converting to meters for standard scientific notation:
$$\lambda_i = 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate initial wavelength in nm.

steps.title: Set up Compton Shift
steps.content:
The shift in wavelength ($\Delta\lambda$) during Compton scattering is given by the formula:

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos \theta)$$

Where:
- $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ (Planck's constant)
- $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$ (mass of a stationary electron)
- $c = 3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ (speed of light)
- $\theta = 60^\circ$ (scattering angle)

steps.title: Calculate Wavelength Shift
steps.content:
Now we substitute the values into the formula to find $\Delta\lambda$. Note that $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$, so $(1 - \cos 60^\circ) = 0.5$.

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta\lambda &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(3.00 \times 10^8)} \times (1 - \cos 60^\circ) \\ \Delta\lambda &= 2.43 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.5 \\ \Delta\lambda &= 1.21 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This shift represents the increase in wavelength due to the energy lost to the electron.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression with constants. Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode.

ti84.instructions: Enter (6.63 * 10^-34) / (9.11 * 10^-31 * 3.00 * 10^8) * (1 - cos(60)). Ensure Mode is set to Degrees.

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula using scientific notation. Toggle the 'deg' button.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength shift.

steps.title: Final Answers
steps.content:
The results for the photon interaction are:
- Initial wavelength: $\lambda_i = 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$
- Wavelength shift: $\Delta\lambda = 1.21 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$

parts.content: Determine the energy of the scattered photon.
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** final wavelength: $\lambda_f = \lambda_i + \Delta\lambda = 1.55 \times 10^{-11} + 1.21 \times 10^{-12} = 1.67 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** final energy: $E_f = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_f} = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^8}{1.67 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.19 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept answer in keV: 74 keV</note>

steps.title: Convert energy to initial wavelength
steps.content:
To find the initial wavelength $\lambda_i$, we use the relationship between energy and wavelength for a photon. First, convert the initial energy from keV to Joules:

$$E_i = 80 \text{ keV} \times 10^3 \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}) = 1.28 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

Now, use the energy formula from the data booklet to solve for $\lambda_i$:

$$\begin{aligned} E &= \frac{hc}{\lambda} \\ \lambda_i &= \frac{hc}{E_i} \\ \lambda_i &= \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3.00 \times 10^8)}{1.28 \times 10^{-14}} \\ \lambda_i &\approx 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 80 * 10^3 * 1.6E-19 to get Energy in Joules. Then, enter (6.63E-34 * 3E8) / Ans to find wavelength.

calculator.description: Convert keV to Joules and find the wavelength.

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength shift
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the Compton wavelength shift $\Delta \lambda$ using the scattering angle. This formula is found in **Topic E.2 (Quantum Physics)** of your HL data booklet:

$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos \theta)$$

Given the scattering angle is $60^\circ$:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta \lambda &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.00 \times 10^8} \times (1 - \cos 60^\circ) \\ \Delta \lambda &\approx (2.43 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}) \times (1 - 0.5) \\ \Delta \lambda &= 1.21 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the fraction template for h / (me * c) and multiply by (1 - cos(60)). Check degree mode.

ti84.instructions: Enter (6.63E-34 / (9.11E-31 * 3E8)) * (1 - cos(60)). Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength shift using the Compton formula.

steps.title: Find the final wavelength
steps.content:
Now, we find the final wavelength $\lambda_f$ of the scattered photon by adding the shift to the initial wavelength:

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_f &= \lambda_i + \Delta \lambda \\ \lambda_f &= 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} + 1.21 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} \\ \lambda_f &= 1.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Notice how the wavelength increased (and energy decreased) after scattering.

gdc.instructions: Sum the previous results stored in your calculator memory.

ti84.instructions: Add your results from the previous two calculations: 1.55E-11 + 1.21E-12.

calculator.description: Sum the initial wavelength and the shift.

steps.title: Calculate the scattered energy
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the new wavelength back into energy. 

$$\begin{aligned} E_f &= \frac{hc}{\lambda_f} \\ E_f &= \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3.00 \times 10^8)}{1.67 \times 10^{-11}} \\ E_f &\approx 1.19 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

To give the final answer in keV (as often seen in IB markschemes):

$$E_f = \frac{1.19 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}}{1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}} \approx 7.4 \times 10^4 \text{ eV} = 74 \text{ keV}$$

According to the markscheme, the answer is worth 2 marks: one for the final wavelength ($1.67 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$) and one for the final energy in Joules or keV.

gdc.instructions: Calculate (hc / λf). Then convert to keV by dividing by (e * 1000).

ti84.instructions: Enter (6.63E-34 * 3E8) / 1.67E-11 to get Joules. Then divide by 1.6E-19 and 1000 to get keV.

calculator.description: Convert the final wavelength to energy in Joules and keV.

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
From the question, we have the following values for the incoming X-ray photon and the scattering event:

*   Initial energy ($E_i$): $80\text{ keV}$
*   Scattering angle ($\theta$): $60^\circ$
*   The target is a stationary electron.

steps.title: State the energy relationship
steps.content:
To find the final energy directly, we use the Compton scattering formula rearranged in terms of energy:

$$\frac{1}{E_f} - \frac{1}{E_i} = \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{m_e c^2}$$

Where:
*   $E_f$ is the energy of the scattered photon.
*   $m_e c^2$ is the rest mass energy of the electron, which is approximately $511\text{ keV}$.

steps.title: Substitute the known values
steps.content:
Rearrange the formula to solve for $\frac{1}{E_f}$ and substitute $E_i = 80\text{ keV}$, $\theta = 60^\circ$, and $m_e c^2 = 511\text{ keV}$:

$$\frac{1}{E_f} = \frac{1}{80} + \frac{1 - \cos(60^\circ)}{511}$$

Since $\cos(60^\circ) = 0.5$, the equation becomes:

$$\frac{1}{E_f} = \frac{1}{80} + \frac{0.5}{511}$$

steps.title: Calculate using a GDC
steps.content: Now, use your calculator to find the value of $\frac{1}{E_f}$ and then take its reciprocal to find $E_f$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Calculate 1/80 + (1 - cos(60))/511.
2. Take the reciprocal of the result (1/Ans).

ti84.instructions:
1. Enter 1/80 + (1 - cos(60))/511 (ensure MODE is in DEGREE).
2. Press ENTER.
3. Press x^-1 (reciprocal button) then ENTER.

desmos.instructions:
1. Set angle to Degrees in settings.
2. Enter the expression: 1 / (1/80 + (1 - cos(60)) / 511).

calculator.description: Calculate the final energy by evaluating the sum and taking the reciprocal.

steps.title: State final energy
steps.content:
From the calculation:

$$\frac{1}{E_f} \approx 0.01348\text{ keV}^{-1}$$

$$E_f = \frac{1}{0.01348} \approx 74.2\text{ keV}$$

Rounding to appropriate significant figures, the energy of the scattered photon is **$74\text{ keV}$**.

parts.content: Discuss how this experiment validated the particle theory of light and its implications for radiation therapy.
parts.markscheme:
- **Discuss** validation: The experiment demonstrates the Compton effect where photons behave as particles colliding with electrons, transferring energy and momentum <marks>A1</marks>
- **Discuss** implications: The scattered photons have lower energy (reducing effectiveness) and change direction, potentially damaging healthy tissue outside the target area <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Validating Particle Theory
steps.content:
The interaction described in the problem, where an X-ray photon collides with a stationary electron and scatters at an angle, is known as the **Compton effect**. This experiment validates the **particle theory of light** because it shows the photon behaving like a discrete particle (or "quantum") that transfers both momentum and energy to the electron during a collision. Classical wave theory cannot explain this discrete transfer, which is a core concept in **Topic E.2: Quantum Physics (HL)**.

$$E = hf$$

steps.title: Therapeutic Implications
steps.content:
In the context of radiation therapy, the Compton effect has two significant implications:

1.  **Reduced Effectiveness**: Since the scattered photon transfers energy to the electron, it leaves the collision with **lower energy**. This makes it less effective at ionizing DNA and destroying target cancer cells.
2.  **Collateral Damage**: Because the photons **change direction** (scattered at an angle), they no longer travel in the precise beam path intended by the oncologist. This allows radiation to reach and potentially damage healthy tissue outside the targeted tumor area.

steps.title: Validation of Particle Model
steps.content: The Compton scattering experiment validates the particle theory of light by treating the X-ray photon as a discrete particle rather than a continuous wave. In a classical wave model, the frequency of the radiation should not change based on the angle of scattering. However, this experiment shows that the photon behaves like a "billiard ball" in a discrete collision with an electron, which is a key concept in **Topic E.2: Quantum Physics**.

highlights.text: collides with a stationary electron and is scattered at an angle of ${60^\circ}$

steps.title: Conservation of Energy/Momentum
steps.content:
The particle model is validated because the interaction follows the laws of conservation of momentum and energy. When the photon strikes the electron, it transfers a portion of its energy and momentum to it. 

Because the photon loses energy ($$E = hf$$), its frequency decreases (and wavelength increases). This specific shift in wavelength depends entirely on the scattering angle, which can only be explained if light possesses particle-like momentum ($$p = \frac{E}{c}$$).

steps.title: Medical Therapy Implications
steps.content:
In radiation therapy, this effect has two critical implications:
1. **Unintended Dose Distribution**: Because the photon changes direction ($$60^\circ$$), it may travel away from the cancerous tumor and strike healthy surrounding tissue, causing unwanted damage.
2. **Reduced Effectiveness**: The scattered photon has lower energy than the initial $$80 \text{ keV}$$. This reduced energy makes the photon less effective at ionizing DNA in the target cells, potentially reducing the treatment's success at that specific site.

parts.content: Explain, using the photoelectric effect, why a higher frequency of light leads to a higher stopping potential.
parts.markscheme:
- Each photon transfers energy $E=h f$ to an electron. <marks>M1</marks>
- Higher frequency means more energy per photon, so ejected electrons have higher kinetic energy and require a higher stopping potential to stop. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Photon Energy and Frequency
steps.content:
To understand the photoelectric effect, we first treat light as a stream of particles called photons. The energy $E$ of a single photon is directly proportional to the frequency $f$ of the light, expressed by the formula:

$$E = hf$$

This means that light with a **higher frequency** consists of photons that carry **more energy**.

steps.title: Transfer to Kinetic Energy
steps.content:
When a photon strikes the metal surface, it transfers its entire energy to a single electron. Some of this energy is used to overcome the metal's work function (the binding energy), and any "leftover" energy becomes the **maximum kinetic energy** ($E_{k,max}$) of the ejected electron.

Because higher frequency light has photons with more energy, the electrons they eject will have significantly more kinetic energy.

steps.title: Applying the Stopping Potential
steps.content:
The stopping potential ($V$) is the voltage needed to create an electric field strong enough to halt even the fastest-moving electrons. 

Mathematically, the work done by the stopping potential must equal the maximum kinetic energy of the electrons ($eV = E_{k,max}$). Since higher frequency light produces electrons with **greater kinetic energy**, a **higher stopping potential** is required to bring them to rest and reduce the photocurrent to zero.

highlights.text: higher frequency of light leads to a higher stopping potential

steps.title: Define photon energy
steps.content:
In the photoelectric effect, light is treated as a stream of particles called photons. According to the IB Physics data booklet, the energy of a single photon is directly proportional to its frequency $f$, as shown by the equation:

$$E = hf$$

Where $h$ is Planck's constant. This means that as the frequency of the incident light increases, each individual photon carries more energy.

steps.title: Apply energy conservation
steps.content:
When a photon hits the metal surface, it transfers its entire energy to a single electron. This energy is used in two ways: 
1. To overcome the **work function** ($\Phi$), which is the minimum energy required to liberate an electron from the metal surface.
2. The remaining energy becomes the **maximum kinetic energy** ($E_{k, \text{max}}$) of the emitted electron.

Algebraically, we represent this conservation of energy as:

$$hf = \Phi + E_{k, \text{max}}$$

steps.title: Relate to stopping potential
steps.content:
The **stopping potential** ($V$) is the voltage required to stop even the most energetic electrons from reaching the collector. The work done by the electric field to stop an electron with charge $e$ must equal its maximum kinetic energy:

$$E_{k, \text{max}} = eV$$

Substituting this into our energy conservation equation gives us Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$$hf = \Phi + eV$$

steps.title: Analyze the relationship
steps.content:
For a specific metal surface, the work function $\Phi$ is a constant value. From the equation $$hf = \Phi + eV$$, we can see that if the frequency $f$ increases, the total energy available ($hf$) also increases.

Since $\Phi$ remains the same, the extra energy must result in a higher maximum kinetic energy for the electrons. Consequently, a larger stopping potential $V$ is needed to bring these faster electrons to rest.

parts.content: What does the shape of the graph suggest about the relationship between stopping potential and frequency?
parts.markscheme: - Linear relationship (or $V$ increases linearly with $f$). <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze frequency increments
steps.content:
To determine the relationship between the two variables, we first look at how the frequency $f$ changes throughout the table. 

Looking at the first two rows, the change in frequency ($\Delta f$) is:
$$5.0 - 4.5 = 0.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$

If we check the subsequent rows, we see this increment remains constant at $0.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$ for every step in the data set.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between consecutive frequency values.

steps.title: Analyze potential increments
steps.content:
Next, we examine the corresponding changes in the stopping potential $V$. 

For the same first two rows, the change in stopping potential ($\Delta V$) is:
$$0.3 - 0.1 = 0.2 \text{ V}$$

Scanning the rest of the table, every time the frequency increases by $0.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$, the stopping potential increases by exactly $0.2 \text{ V}$. Since equal changes in the independent variable result in equal changes in the dependent variable, the gradient (slope) of the relationship is constant.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between consecutive stopping potential values.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
A relationship with a constant gradient is represented by a straight line. In physics and mathematics, this indicates that the two variables have a **linear relationship**.

You can visualize this by imagining the points plotted on a graph; they would align perfectly to form a straight line with the equation format $y = mx + c$.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content: Based on the constant increments found in the data table, the shape of the graph suggests a **linear relationship** between the stopping potential $V$ and the frequency $f$.

steps.title: Recall the photoelectric equation
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between stopping potential ($V$) and frequency ($f$), we start with Einstein's photoelectric equation from **Topic E: Nuclear and quantum physics**. This equation describes the conservation of energy during the photoelectric effect:

$$E_{max} = hf - \Phi$$

Where:
- $E_{max}$ is the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons.
- $h$ is Planck's constant (found in the data booklet).
- $f$ is the frequency of incident light.
- $\Phi$ is the work function of the metal (the minimum energy required to eject an electron).

steps.title: Relate energy to potential
steps.content:
The stopping potential $V$ is defined as the potential difference required to stop the most energetic electrons. Therefore, the maximum kinetic energy can be expressed in terms of the elementary charge $e$ and the stopping potential:

$$E_{max} = eV$$

Substituting this into our primary equation gives:

$$eV = hf - \Phi$$

steps.title: Rearrange into linear form
steps.content:
To see how $V$ depends on $f$, we rearrange the equation to solve for $V$ by dividing every term by the charge $e$:

$$V = \left( \frac{h}{e} \right) f - \frac{\Phi}{e}$$

This equation matches the general form of a straight line:

$$y = mx + c$$

Where:
- $y$ is the stopping potential $V$.
- $x$ is the frequency $f$.
- $m$ (the gradient) is $\frac{h}{e}$.
- $c$ (the y-intercept) is $-\frac{\Phi}{e}$.

steps.title: Verify with provided data
steps.content:
Let's check if the data in the table follows this linear pattern by calculating the change in $V$ relative to the change in $f$ ($\Delta V / \Delta f$) for the first two entries:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{0.3 - 0.1}{5.0 - 4.5} = \frac{0.2}{0.5} = 0.4 \text{ V}/(10^{14} \text{ Hz})$$

If we check any other pair, such as the last two, we get the same constant rate of change, confirming the relationship is linear.

gdc.instructions: Divide the total change in V by the total change in f: (1.1 - 0.1) / (7.0 - 4.5).

ti84.instructions: Calculate the slope between the first and last data points: (1.1 - 0.1) / (7.0 - 4.5).

calculator.description: Verify the slope is constant across the data table.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Since the equation $V = \frac{h}{e}f - \frac{\Phi}{e}$ is a first-order polynomial (the variables are to the power of 1) and the data shows a constant rate of change, the shape of the graph is a straight line.

Therefore, the relationship is a **linear relationship** (or $V$ increases linearly with $f$).

parts.content: Use your graph to determine the gradient and estimate Planck's constant $h$.
parts.markscheme:
- Gradient $\approx \frac{1.0}{2.5} = 0.4 \mathrm{~V} /\left(10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}\right)$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Estimate $h=e \times$ gradient $=1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.4 \times 10^{-14}=6.4 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Link Physics to the Graph
steps.content:
To find Planck's constant from the graph, we first need to look at the photoelectric effect equation. The energy of the incident photon ($hf$) equals the work function ($\Phi$) plus the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron ($E_{k,max}$).

Since $E_{k,max} = eV$, where $V$ is the stopping potential, we can write:

$$eV = hf - \Phi$$

Rearranging for $V$ gives us a linear equation in the form $y = mx + c$:

$$V = \left(\frac{h}{e}\right)f - \frac{\Phi}{e}$$

This tells us that the gradient ($m$) of our graph is equal to $\frac{h}{e}$.

steps.title: Calculate the Gradient
steps.content:
The gradient is calculated as the change in stopping potential ($ΔV$) divided by the change in frequency ($Δf$). Let's use the two extreme data points from the table: $(4.5 \times 10^{14}, 0.1)$ and $(7.0 \times 10^{14}, 1.1)$.

$$\text{Gradient } (m) = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{f_2 - f_1}$$

$$\Delta V = 1.1\text{ V} - 0.1\text{ V} = 1.0\text{ V}$$

$$\Delta f = (7.0 - 4.5) \times 10^{14}\text{ Hz} = 2.5 \times 10^{14}\text{ Hz}$$

$$\text{Gradient } (m) = \frac{1.0}{2.5 \times 10^{14}} = 0.4 \times 10^{-14}\text{ V/Hz}$$

desmos.instructions: Type 1.0 / 2.5 to find the decimal value.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio for the gradient.

steps.title: Estimate Planck's Constant
steps.content:
Now that we have the gradient $m = \frac{h}{e}$, we can find $h$ by multiplying the gradient by the elementary charge $e$. 

From the data booklet, the elementary charge is:
$$e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$

Substitute the values to find $h$:

$$h = m \times e$$

$$h = (0.4 \times 10^{-14}) \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19})$$

$$h = 0.64 \times 10^{-33} = 6.4 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply 0.4 by 1.60. Then combine the exponents manually (-14 + -19 = -33).

calculator.description: Multiply the gradient numerical part by the elementary charge.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The gradient of the line is approximately $0.4 \times 10^{-14} \text{ V/Hz}$ (or $4.0 \times 10^{-15} \text{ V/Hz}$). 

Using this gradient, we estimate Planck's constant to be:
$$h \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$$

This value is very close to the standard accepted value of $6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ found in your data booklet!

parts.content: Determine the threshold frequency $f_{0}$ for this metal and estimate the work function $\phi$.
parts.markscheme:
- Extrapolate graph to find $V=0 \Rightarrow f_{0} \approx 4.25 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Use $\phi=h f_{0}=6.4 \times 10^{-34} \times 4.25 \times 10^{14}=2.7 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Understand the graphical relationship
steps.content:
In the photoelectric effect, Einstein's equation relates the maximum kinetic energy of electrons ($E_{max} = eV$) to the energy of incident photons ($hf$) and the work function ($\phi$):

$$eV = hf - \phi$$

Rearranging for stopping potential $V$:

$$V = \frac{h}{e}f - \frac{\phi}{e}$$

This is a linear equation of the form $y = mx + c$. The **threshold frequency** ($f_0$) occurs when the stopping potential is zero ($V=0$), meaning the photons have just enough energy to eject electrons but with zero kinetic energy.

steps.title: Identify the threshold frequency
steps.content:
Using the **Graphical Extrapolation** method, we look for the frequency where the line of best fit crosses the x-axis ($V=0$). 

Looking at the data, the potential increases by $0.2\text{ V}$ for every $0.5 \times 10^{14}\text{ Hz}$ increase in frequency. If we move backwards from the first data point $(4.5, 0.1)$:

- At $f = 4.5 \times 10^{14}\text{ Hz}$, $V = 0.1\text{ V}$.
- Since $0.1\text{ V}$ is half of the $0.2\text{ V}$ step, we subtract half of the frequency step ($0.25 \times 10^{14}\text{ Hz}$).

$$f_0 = 4.5 \times 10^{14} - 0.25 \times 10^{14} = 4.25 \times 10^{14}\text{ Hz}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Enter the data into Spreadsheet/List.
2. Perform a Linear Regression to find $V = mf + c$.
3. Solve for $f$ when $V = 0$.

1. Press [STAT] [1:Edit] and enter frequencies in L1 and potentials in L2.
2. Press [STAT] [CALC] [4:LinReg(ax+b)].
3. Note the equation: $y = 0.4x - 1.7$. 
4. Solve $0 = 0.4x - 1.7$.

2. Use the regression model $y_1 \sim mx_1 + b$.
3. Find the x-intercept of the resulting line.

calculator.description: Find the x-intercept using a linear model.

steps.title: Calculate the work function
steps.content:
The work function $\phi$ is the energy required to remove an electron from the metal surface. It is related to the threshold frequency by the formula found in the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet:

$$\phi = h f_0$$

Using the Planck constant $h$ from the data booklet:

$$\phi = (6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (4.25 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz})$$

$$\phi \approx 2.82 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (as per the input data), we get $2.8 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the Planck constant by the threshold frequency.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the product.

calculator.description: Calculate the work function in Joules.

steps.title: Final Answers
steps.content:
Based on the graphical extrapolation and subsequent calculation:

- **Threshold frequency**: $f_0 = 4.25 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$
- **Work function**: $\phi \approx 2.8 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

(Note: Small variations in the final digit of the work function may occur depending on the exact value used for $h$ or the precision of the line of best fit extrapolation).

steps.title: Establish the linear relationship
steps.content:
To analyze the photoelectric effect algebraically, we start with Einstein’s photoelectric equation:

$$hf = \phi + E_{k,max}$$

Since the stopping potential $V$ is related to the maximum kinetic energy by $E_{k,max} = eV$, we can rewrite this as:

$$hf = \phi + eV$$

Rearranging for $V$ gives us the linear equation for our analysis:

$$V = \left( \frac{h}{e} \right) f - \frac{\phi}{e}$$

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
Using the data points from the table, we calculate the gradient ($m$) of the $V$ vs $f$ relationship. Let's use the first two points: $(4.5 \times 10^{14}, 0.1)$ and $(5.0 \times 10^{14}, 0.3)$.

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta f} \\ m &= \frac{0.3 - 0.1}{(5.0 - 4.5) \times 10^{14}} \\ m &= \frac{0.2}{0.5 \times 10^{14}} \\ m &= 4.0 \times 10^{-15} \text{ V/Hz} \end{aligned}$$

This gradient represents the ratio $\frac{h}{e}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the change in V divided by the change in f.

calculator.description: Calculate the slope (gradient) from the first two data points.

steps.title: Determine threshold frequency
steps.content:
The threshold frequency $f_0$ is the frequency at which the stopping potential is exactly zero ($V=0$). We can find this by substituting one point and the gradient into the equation $V - V_1 = m(f - f_1)$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 - 0.1 &= 4.0 \times 10^{-15}(f_0 - 4.5 \times 10^{14}) \\ -0.1 &= 4.0 \times 10^{-15}f_0 - 1.8 \\ 1.7 &= 4.0 \times 10^{-15}f_0 \\ f_0 &= \frac{1.7}{4.0 \times 10^{-15}} \\ f_0 &= 4.25 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the constant by the gradient to find f0.

calculator.description: Solve for the threshold frequency f0 when stopping potential V = 0.

steps.title: Estimate the work function
steps.content:
The work function $\phi$ is given by the formula $\phi = hf_0$. Using Planck's constant from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} \phi &= (6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (4.25 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}) \\ \phi &\approx 2.82 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Alternatively, since $\phi/e$ is the magnitude of the $y$-intercept ($1.7 \text{ V}$):

$$\begin{aligned} \phi &= e \times 1.7 \text{ V} \\ \phi &= (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) \times 1.7 \text{ V} \\ \phi &= 2.72 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two significant figures as per the markscheme, we get **$2.7 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$**.

gdc.instructions: Multiply elementary charge by the intercept voltage.

calculator.description: Calculate the work function using the charge of an electron and the y-intercept value.

steps.title: Identify the photoelectric equation
steps.content:
To solve this using simultaneous equations, we start with the fundamental photoelectric effect equation. In this experiment, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons ($E_{max}$) is equal to the energy of the incident photon ($hf$) minus the work function ($\phi$) of the metal. 

Since the stopping potential $V$ is the voltage required to stop the most energetic electrons, we know that $E_{max} = eV$. We can combine these into the following linear equation:

$$eV = hf - \phi$$

This relates the stopping potential $V$ to the frequency $f$. Note that we will calculate $h$ (Planck's constant) experimentally from our data points rather than using the standard value, as simulated data often has slight variations.

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
We need to select two data points from the table to create our system of equations. Let's choose the first and last points for the best accuracy:
1. $f_1 = 4.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}, V_1 = 0.1 \text{ V}$
2. $f_2 = 7.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}, V_2 = 1.1 \text{ V}$

Using the elementary charge $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, we substitute these into $eV = hf - \phi$:

$$\begin{aligned} (1.60 \times 10^{-19})(0.1) &= h(4.5 \times 10^{14}) - \phi \quad (1) \\ (1.60 \times 10^{-19})(1.1) &= h(7.0 \times 10^{14}) - \phi \quad (2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for Planck's constant
steps.content:
To eliminate $\phi$, subtract equation (1) from equation (2):

$$\begin{aligned} e(1.1 - 0.1) &= h(7.0 \times 10^{14} - 4.5 \times 10^{14}) \\ e(1.0) &= h(2.5 \times 10^{14}) \end{aligned}$$

Solving for $h$:

$$h = \frac{1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C} \cdot 1.0 \text{ V}}{2.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}} = 6.4 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$$

This experimental value of $h$ is slightly different from the data booklet value, which is common in lab-based questions.

steps.title: Calculate the work function
steps.content:
Now substitute $h = 6.4 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ back into equation (1) to find the work function $\phi$:

$$\begin{aligned} (1.60 \times 10^{-19})(0.1) &= (6.4 \times 10^{-34})(4.5 \times 10^{14}) - \phi \\ 0.16 \times 10^{-19} &= 2.88 \times 10^{-19} - \phi \\ \phi &= 2.88 \times 10^{-19} - 0.16 \times 10^{-19} \\ \phi &= 2.72 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two significant figures, we get $\phi \approx 2.7 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

steps.title: Determine threshold frequency
steps.content:
The threshold frequency $f_0$ occurs when the stopping potential is zero ($V = 0$). At this point, the energy of the photon is exactly equal to the work function:

$$hf_0 = \phi$$

$$f_0 = \frac{\phi}{h}$$

Using our values:

$$f_0 = \frac{2.72 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{6.4 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}} = 4.25 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the threshold frequency by dividing the work function by the experimental Planck's constant.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The threshold frequency is $f_0 = 4.25 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$ and the estimated work function is $\phi = 2.7 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

In an IB exam, you would earn:
- 1 mark for correctly determining $f_0$ (either by simultaneous equations or graph extrapolation).
- 1 mark for correctly calculating $\phi$.

parts.content: Suggest one assumption in the analysis that might not hold true in a real experiment.
parts.markscheme: - Assumes all electrons absorb energy from a single photon and there is no energy loss in the material (for the most energetic electrons). <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Understand the ideal model
steps.content:
The photoelectric effect analysis is based on Einstein's equation, which relates the energy of the incident photon to the work function and the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons ($E_{k, \text{max}}$). This maximum kinetic energy is what we measure using the stopping potential ($V$):

$$hf = \Phi + eV$$

This equation represents an ideal scenario where the electrons that are easiest to remove (those at the very surface) escape with the maximum possible energy.

steps.title: Consider energy loss mechanisms
steps.content:
In a real metal, electrons are not just on the surface; they are distributed throughout the lattice of metal ions. When an electron deep within the metal absorbs a photon, it must travel through the material to reach the surface.

During this journey, the electron is likely to collide with metal ions or other electrons. From an **energy loss perspective**, each collision causes the electron to lose some of its kinetic energy to the lattice before it can escape.

steps.title: Identify the assumption
steps.content:
The analysis assumes that the most energetic electrons—those we are measuring to find the stopping potential—reach the surface without losing any of their kinetic energy through these collisions.

In a real experiment, even the most energetic electrons might have lost a small amount of energy, or the surface itself might not be perfectly "clean," leading to variations that the ideal model doesn't account for.

highlights.text: no energy loss in the material (for the most energetic electrons)

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The key assumption is that there is **no energy loss** in the material for the most energetic electrons. 

This falls under **Topic E.2: Quantum physics**, where we learn that the photoelectric equation specifically describes the *maximum* possible kinetic energy, assuming a perfect transfer of energy from a single photon to a single electron without external losses.

steps.title: The 1-to-1 Interaction Model
steps.content:
In the standard analysis of the photoelectric effect, we use the **Photon Interaction Perspective**. This assumes a strict one-to-one relationship: exactly one photon interacts with exactly one electron to provide the energy required for escape.

$$E = hf$$

This model simplifies reality by assuming that energy isn't shared between multiple electrons and that electrons don't absorb energy from multiple photons simultaneously.

steps.title: Assuming Zero Energy Loss
steps.content:
When we measure the stopping potential $V$, we are specifically looking for the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{k, \text{max}}$) of the emitted electrons. The analysis assumes that these "fastest" electrons lose **no energy** to the metal's internal structure (like collisions with metal ions) on their way out.

In a real experiment, even the most energetic electrons might lose a small amount of energy to the lattice before escaping the surface.

steps.title: Identify the Specific Assumption
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, the key assumption that might fail in reality is that all electrons absorb energy from a single photon without any energy loss within the material.

In practice, factors like surface impurities or internal scattering mean that the ideal $E_{k, \text{max}}$ predicted by the equation might be slightly higher than what is actually measured.

highlights.text: Assumes all electrons absorb energy from a single photon and there is no energy loss in the material (for the most energetic electrons).

steps.title: Identify the ideal assumption
steps.content: In the simulation, the analysis relies on the **Surface Uniformity Perspective**. This assumes that the light is directed onto a perfectly **clean metal surface**, where every part of the surface reacts to the incident light in exactly the same way.

steps.title: Evaluate real-world conditions
steps.content:
In a real laboratory experiment, it is extremely difficult to maintain a perfectly uniform surface. Real metals often have:

1.  **Oxide layers**: Exposure to air causes a layer of oxidation.
2.  **Impurities**: Microscopic contaminants can be present on the surface.
3.  **Structural defects**: Different crystal orientations across the surface can exist.

These factors mean the **work function** (the minimum energy to remove an electron) may not be constant across the entire surface.

steps.title: Relate to energy loss
steps.content:
The simulation assumes that the most energetic electrons escape without any extra energy loss. However, if the surface is not uniform or clean, electrons might lose varying amounts of energy as they exit the material due to collisions or varying potential barriers. 

As the markscheme notes, the analysis assumes **no energy loss in the material** for the most energetic electrons, which is an idealization that rarely holds true in real setups.

steps.title: Final Suggestion
steps.content:
Therefore, a valid assumption to suggest is:

**The assumption that the metal surface is perfectly clean and uniform (or that there is no energy loss for electrons within the material) might not hold true.**

methods.label: Direct Calculation using de Broglie Equation

steps.title: Identify the de Broglie equation
steps.content:
To find the wavelength of a moving particle like an electron, we use the de Broglie relationship. This concept is covered in **Topic E.2: Quantum Physics**. The formula relates the wavelength $\lambda$ to the momentum $p$ of the particle:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv}$$

Where:
- $h$ is the Planck constant
- $m$ is the mass of the electron
- $v$ is the velocity of the electron

steps.title: Identify constants and values
steps.content:
From the **Physical Constants** section of your data booklet and the question specification, we have:
- Planck constant: $$h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$$
- Velocity: $$v = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$
- Mass of an electron: $$m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$$

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength
steps.content:
Substitute these values into the de Broglie equation to calculate the wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 2 \times 10^6}$$

First, calculate the momentum ($mv$) in the denominator:
$$p = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 2 \times 10^6 = 1.822 \times 10^{-24} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$$

Then divide $h$ by this result:
$$\lambda \approx 3.64 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The de Broglie wavelength of the electron is:

$$\lambda = 3.64 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To find the de Broglie wavelength, we first need to identify the mass of an electron and its velocity. From the question and the data booklet, we have:

- Mass of an electron ($m$): $9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$
- Velocity ($v$): $2 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
- Planck’s constant ($h$): $6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$

steps.title: Calculate linear momentum
steps.content:
First, we calculate the linear momentum ($p$) of the electron using the formula from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$p = mv$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} p &= (9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}) \times (2 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1}) \\ p &= 1.822 \times 10^{-24} \text{ kg m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply de Broglie relation
steps.content:
Now, we use the de Broglie wavelength relationship. Although the specific formula $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$ is a fundamental part of **Topic E.2 (Quantum physics)**, we can substitute our calculated momentum into it:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{1.822 \times 10^{-24} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}} \\ \lambda &\approx 3.64 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The de Broglie wavelength of the electron is **$3.64 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$**.

According to the markscheme:
- **1 mark** is awarded for the correct substitution of values into the wavelength equation.
- **1 mark** is awarded for the correct final answer with appropriate units.

steps.title: Recall the wavelength formula
steps.content:
To compare the wave behavior of these particles, we use the de Broglie wavelength formula, which relates the wavelength ($\lambda$) of a particle to its momentum ($p = mv$). This concept is covered in **Topic E.2: Quantum physics**.

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv}$$

steps.title: Calculate electron wavelength
steps.content:
First, we calculate the wavelength of the electron using its mass ($m_e \approx 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$), the given velocity, and Planck's constant ($h$) from the data booklet.

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_e &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}) \times (2 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1})} \\ \lambda_e &\approx 3.64 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the value typically used for an electron at this specific velocity.

steps.title: Calculate proton wavelength
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the wavelength for a proton moving at the same speed. We substitute the mass of a proton ($m_p \approx 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$) into the same formula.

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_p &= \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{(1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}) \times (2 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1})} \\ \lambda_p &\approx 1.99 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the comparison ratio
steps.content:
To compare them directly, we find the ratio of the electron wavelength to the proton wavelength:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p} = \frac{3.64 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}}{1.99 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}} \approx 1830$$

Because the proton is roughly $1836$ times more massive than the electron, its de Broglie wavelength is significantly smaller. Specifically, the electron's wavelength is about $1800$ times larger than that of the proton at this speed.

calculator.description: Find the ratio of the two wavelengths.

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To compare the wavelengths, we start with the de Broglie wavelength formula from the **Nuclear and quantum physics** section (Topic E.2):

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv}$$

Where:
- $h$ is Planck's constant
- $m$ is the mass of the particle
- $v$ is the velocity

steps.title: Establish the proportionality
steps.content:
In this problem, we are told the proton and electron move at the same speed. Since $h$ is a fundamental constant and the velocity $v$ is the same for both particles, the product $\lambda \cdot m$ must be a constant.

This means the wavelength is inversely proportional to the mass:

$$\lambda \propto \frac{1}{m}$$

steps.title: Set up the ratio
steps.content:
Using the inverse relationship, we can compare the electron wavelength ($\lambda_e$) to the proton wavelength ($\lambda_p$) by looking at the ratio of their masses:

$$\frac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p} = \frac{m_p}{m_e}$$

Because the proton is significantly heavier than the electron ($m_p \approx 1836 \times m_e$), its wavelength will be significantly smaller.

steps.title: Calculate the comparison factor
steps.content:
Using the mass of a proton ($1.67 \times 10^{-27}$ kg) and the mass of an electron (approx. $9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg):

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}} \approx 1833$$

This confirms that the electron's wavelength is about 1800 times larger than the proton's wavelength at the same speed.

highlights.text: The electron wavelength is larger by a factor of $\approx 1800$

steps.title: Final Comparison
steps.content:
By substituting the velocity into the formula for the proton, we find:
$$\lambda_p = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27} \times 2 \times 10^6} \approx 1.99 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}$$

Comparing this to the electron's wavelength ($\approx 3.64 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$), we see the electron's wavelength is indeed much larger, specifically by a factor of the mass ratio.

steps.title: Identify the de Broglie relation
steps.content:
To calculate the wavelength of a particle like an electron, we use the de Broglie equation. This equation relates the wave-like property of the electron (wavelength $\lambda$) to its particle-like property (momentum $p$). 

This concept is a key part of **Topic E.2: Quantum Physics**. The formula is:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

where $h$ is Planck's constant and $p$ is the momentum.

steps.title: Substitute the given values

$$\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{3.0 \times 10^{-24}}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final wavelength

$$\lambda = 2.21 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$$

This calculation would typically earn you the final accuracy mark in an IB exam. Note that the unit for wavelength is meters (m).

steps.title: Observe the diffraction pattern
steps.content: When a beam of electrons is directed at a thin material, such as a crystal lattice, they do not simply pass through or bounce off like tiny billiard balls. Instead, they spread out and form an interference or diffraction pattern (often seen as concentric rings) on a detector screen.

steps.title: Identify characteristic wave behavior
steps.content:
In physics, **diffraction** and **interference** are unique signatures of waves. These phenomena occur when wave fronts encounter an obstacle or an opening (like the gaps between atoms in a crystal) that is similar in size to their wavelength. 

You can review these classical wave properties in **Topic C.3 (Wave phenomena)**.

steps.title: Connect to matter waves
steps.content: Because electrons (which have mass and charge and were traditionally viewed as particles) produce these specific patterns, we must conclude that they behave like waves in this context. This provides direct experimental evidence for the **wave nature of matter**, confirming the theory of wave-particle duality.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question:
1. Identify that electrons produce **interference or diffraction patterns** when interacting with a crystal lattice (1 mark).
2. State that **diffraction is a wave property**, proving that electrons possess wave-like characteristics (1 mark).

parts.content: Calculate the wavelength shift $\Delta \lambda$ using the Compton formula. (Use $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J\,s}$, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$, $c = 3.00 \times 10^8\ \text{m\,s}^{-1}$.)
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the Compton wavelength: $\frac{h}{m_e c} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.00 \times 10^8} = 2.43 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$ <marks>M1</marks>
Calculate the wavelength shift: $\Delta \lambda = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} (1 - \cos 90^\circ) = 2.43 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m} = 0.00243\ \text{nm}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the Compton formula
steps.content:
To find the wavelength shift $\Delta \lambda$, we use the Compton scattering formula. This formula relates the change in wavelength to the scattering angle $\theta$ and several physical constants:

$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c} (1 - \cos \theta)$$

This concept is part of **Topic E.2 Quantum physics**, which explores the particle-like behavior of light.

steps.title: Substitute the known values
steps.content:
We substitute the constants provided in the question and the scattering angle into the formula. 

Given values:
- $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$
- $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$
- $c = 3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
- $\theta = 90^\circ$

Substituting these into the equation:

$$\Delta \lambda = \left( \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.00 \times 10^8} \right) (1 - \cos 90^\circ)$$

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength shift
steps.content:
First, calculate the constant term $\frac{h}{m_e c}$ (often called the Compton wavelength, $\lambda_C$). Then, evaluate the angular term.

$$\lambda_C = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2.733 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 2.43 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$$

Since $\cos 90^\circ = 0$, the term $(1 - \cos 90^\circ)$ simplifies to $1$. Therefore:

$$\Delta \lambda = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} \times (1 - 0) = 2.43 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$$
